name: OverDrift Festival
review:
Do not buy if you plan on using a steering wheel, at least for now. Wheel support is nearly non-existent and it feels like if an arcade racer tried to make it feel realistic but over tuned all the values. However, the controller feels fine. 
*edit* 
After the most recent update with physics changes wheel drivability is much better and more fun, however still not on the level of games like carX, Asetto or Forza this is a good change, but there are still other issues, here's a list.
1. very little to do in the game 
2. navigation is annoying without waypoints or better GPS
3. cars are locked to pre-customized body kits and all tires are super stretched
4. input mapping is a chore and very limited  
5. car Ai is just annoying and ruins driving on the open road with how narrow the roads are 
6. the car damage system only acts as an annoyance towards players and serves no point except to take players money and extend the already grindy progression

OK this might be long but ive been playing this game for a week an half on a controller and g29 wheel OK so these are some things that can be tweak.

1.Add full controller and wheel support plus accessories (hand brake).

7.add feature to open hood/car doors/and trunks.
8.add feature to create custom driver for in car only.
9.add feature to save tunes.

name: OverDrift Festival
review:
Absolute crap. That is my simplified overall view of this game. It is most definitely not worth the money they are asking for, especially for the additional DLC. For reference, I use a controller with my driving games. I do not use a steering wheel, so I cannot comment on how it is when using one in this game.

Pros:
- Well, it looks good for the most part. But, looking good can only get you so far.
- That's pretty much it.

Cons:
- There are some obvious spelling issues.
- There is no mouse support. So, when it first starts up, you're going to instinctively move the mouse and see about clicking on the whatever servers are available at the time to find that doesn't work. You have to use the arrow keys on your keyboard. This is the first game I've ever encountered that didn't have basic keyboard AND MOUSE support. Why the developers thought this was a smart idea is beyond me, as it makes absolutely no sense whatsoever.
- For me, the game started up in a small window; I'd say it was about 800 x 600. Which made seeing it on my 65" 4k TV difficult to figure out what I was supposed to do to start an actual game (which only showed 3 "sessions/servers" to choose from). I had to hit Ctrl + Enter to force it into full-screen just so I could continue.
- No "main menu" from the start to setup the game to your liking. No, common sense and basic human intelligence were not considered when it came to making this game. You do not gain access to the menu system until after you've picked a session to join.
- The overall main menu is a clusterf**k and looks sloppy/busy.
- The Graphics Settings menu doesn't actually do a proper full-screen ability. You can choose different resolutions to try and make it fill the entire screen, but it's not an actual full-screen. It's more like full windowed mode.
- I could not enable V-sync. It was an option, but it was an option that you could not enable.
- Control mapping is defaulted to the keyboard. This is almost always a sign that the people responsible for this genuinely do not know what they're doing when it comes to making a game. A DRIVING game. With keyboard mapping as default. A DRIVING. GAME. When the usual thing you'd use is a controller or a steering wheel. And, the control mapping menu doesn't even have the ability to give a selection of whether you want to use a keyboard, a controller, or a steering wheel. You can only manually edit each function individually.
- It appears that you cannot freely rotate the camera around. Why? Every other driving game out there can. It's pretty much standard practice. Oh right, I forgot. That whole "common sense and basic human intelligence" bit I spoke about earlier.
- When driving, my car wanted to just veer to the right on its own, with no input from me. Try to get it back inline and it'll just repeat the process. Naturally, steering the car and trying to keep it behaving normally wasn't going to happen. So, there's something going on with this game and having issues with tracking. It seems this is a bit of a known problem on the discussion boards.

That's pretty much it for pros and cons. I haven't put much time into this game because I didn't want to continue. When a game starts up and immediately comes across as a badly put-together attempt with no logical line of thinking involved, that tells me that it is not worth my time to mess with it anymore. A user shouldn't have to trudge through a sea of bulls**t to try and get the game to their liking before they even turn the wheel. There should be common practices already in place to make the first impression a welcoming one. Even the discussion threads show that the developer doesn't really bother addressing issues. And, a few users have commented about how this is another game that is going to die out real soon. I can see why.

OverDrift Festival is what happens when you get people who don't understand what the words "organized" and "thought-out" mean. Poorly laid out menu system, nonsensical mapping selection system, and broken features make this game Early-Access, in my opinion. It's not polished at all.

name: OverDrift Festival
review:
T500RS review:

Mixed feelings for this one. Its a fun game with great gameplay. But its very arcady and the force feedback is not that good. Let me explain:

The autosteering is off, its hard to explain but ill try. The car straightens too much because the autosteering is countersteering too much so you constantly have to "steer" into the corner to stay drifting. (Im not talking about assists or anything like that, if you know you know...) And another thing is that it feels like the autosteer starts to slow during a transition and then the turning speed picks up at the end. (Ive got around 2000h in sim driving, my FFB settings are not the issue).

The rear is not really controlled with throttle but rather using the steeringwheel and the weight transfer is almost nonexistent.
Its hard to explain but its very off from RL. This is not a realistic sim but its playable but it takes some getting used to, car upgrades and some extreme tuning makes it OK. 

Even tho it has issues with FFB i really like this game, if you are thinking of buying it just be aware of this if you are playing with a wheel. 

What makes me like this game is the community and the gameplay. People are really nice and helpful. The maps are amazing. If they just tune the force feedback a little bit this will be a really good game!

As of right now its playable and fun and if they improve the FFB i will update this review.

name: OverDrift Festival
review:
Honestly a really fun game, i love the fact its open world and u can drive/teleport anywhere on the map within seconds, the graphics is great, and the car packs offers a lot of content for what u pay for, although im usualy against pay to win type of packs. Also a huge bonus that the game gets updated quite often and the developers genuinely seem to care to update the game and listen to the community wich is great!

However there is a few major cons about this game i'd like to see fixed/improved in the future. For starters there is massive server desync when u try to tandem with other people, Second of all no body modifications? and third of all when ur making liveries there should be an autosave/manual save feature on unfinished projects so that u don't have to do it all in 1 go. Also no way of sharing liveries with friends wich is sad. And also for cinematic/photo settings it lacks on some features compared to games like CarX and Forza Horizon 4/5.
Also there is no mods for the game wich is kinda sad, that's what makes games like CarX and Assetto Corsa so great so would love to see that in the future.

It will be probably take a while before this game can be compared with something like CarX/Assetto Corsa but im hoping it'll be better with time as it is extremly fun to play

Other than that i can definately recommend picking up the game.

Overall rating of the game : 8/10

review:
Definitely the best drifting game I've played in a while. 
-Selection of cars is amazing
-Customisation is amazing
-Physics feel amazing
-Game looks amazing. 

The only two things i'd change with the game right now are :
-The servers have pretty bad lag compensation (for me being an NA player, it has a couple seconds of desync.)
-The handbrake needs a bit more tweaking as it feels pretty weak right now.

The best part of the game surely has to be the Tandem lead/chase feature. For this price i say you have no reason to not buy it. The livery editor is also pretty nice to work with.  

I give it a solid 8.5/10 as of right now, could easily be a 10 with more updates.

name: OverDrift Festival
review:
A really cool drifting game just for the fun of it.
The community seems to be very friendly. You either encounter people who don't communicate at all or do it in a friendly way. I have yet to encounter anyone who is rude or toxic.

The driving physics feel great. Or should I say drifting physics, since this is what the game is all about?

The customization is nice! You don't have to stick to premade liveries, you can create your very own one ingame.
Lots of decals, wheels, tires, exhausts, etc. to choose from. Sadly no wings though. I'd love to put the iconic Evo2 wing on my 190E.

Money is earned by drifting, either in events or just in freeride.

Why so many negative reviews? 
While the people do have a point about the dlc stuff and having some customization like neon and tire smoke behind a paywall, they are exaggerating badly by saying "almost all cars are behind paywall". That's not true. You can unlock many cars with just the base game. I bought the "overpass" and the car pack that included my dream car. That's all I'll ever need in the game.

The graphics are quite okay, definitely better than comparable drift games though.

The sound design isn't too great. The car sounds are low quality and the music... ugh... It's a matter of your own personal music taste, but I'm grateful for the ability to turn the music volume to 0%. All in game music is some kind of techno dubsteb gangsterrap combined.

It plays very well with a steering wheel and uses 900°, so no worries, no only 180° ratio like in other games.

What definitely needs to be added in my opinion is an option to make the camera automatically look in the direction your drifting in cokpit view. I play only in cockpit view and tandem drifting is basically a gamble because I can't see the other car or even the track. 
Or maybe even a VR option. That would be ideal for me.

All in all probably one of the best dedicated drifting games around!

name: HEXCRAFT: Harlequin Fair
review: Bloodlines meets a tarot infused fever dream

name: HEXCRAFT: Harlequin Fair
review:
Not too long into my first playthrough of [i]Hexcraft: Harlequin Fair[/i] I found myself venturing into a haunting trainyard, the striking view interrupted by the sharpshooting cowgirl trying to gun down a werewolf, an event I decided not to get involved in. Instead, I carefully made my way into an unlocked hotel room where I found assorted photos of deceased president John Fitzgerald Kennedy plastered over the wall, and understandably transfixed by the sight I temporarily forget about the conflict unfolding outside. A bad decision as it turned out, as I am shortly thereafter mauled to death by said werewolf.

"Immersive Sims" are seeing something of a resurgence among indie developers as of late, and none stand out quite as distinct as Hexcraft: Harlequin Fair. If Gloomwood and Peripeteia seek to be the spiritual successors of Thief & Deus Ex, Hexcraft feels like the result of Ultima's legacy taking an entirely different direction, as if ImSims as a genre strove more to be like Pathologic than any kind of player empowerment sandbox.

The central crux of the Hexcraft games is that every unique named npc acts autonomously in the game world. They each have hidden stats & abilities, as well as a variety of personality traits and localised knowledge about the present game state. Using this information they'll act on their goals, leading to all kinds of dynamic situations. Sometimes you'll enter an area to an unfolding gunfight, or enter a zone to find it under control of a faction that weren't present last time you were there: all emergent outcomes of the game's simulation. Far more importantly, sometimes in the pursuit of progression-relevant items you'll find they've already been snagged by another npc, leaving you to do the detective work of ascertaining who-has-what and facilitating a way to get it back.

To do this you will have to explore the world, and take advantage of every boon you can find. Besides the various weapons you can acquire there's various forms of equipment and consumable items scattered about the world or available to craft that will bolster your stats so you can actually survive combat, and reasonably stand a chance in the game's various dungeons. Truly diligent explorers will find various magic skills hidden in the world that have utility ranging from freely unlocking doors to divining which npc has hold of an item you need. Of course at the end of it all the most effective tool you have is the very characters around you. Sometimes the best way to solve a problem is to simply facilitate a conflict and take advantage in the aftermath. If you ever find yourself struggling to kill someone then hey - get someone else to do it for you.

It's this interplay that makes Harlequin Fair such an interesting experience, the world will not wait for you to act but it will respond to your actions, and no two playthroughs will be the same as a result. And importantly this is all presented in a delightfully old-school fashion. At no point will the game proactively tell you what you have to do or even how to do it, you're just left to intuit as you check your home computer for live updates on the situation around the map, interact with the other characters, and slowly wade through the chaos unfolding - piecing together your goals and then acting on them.

Hexcraft is one of those rare games that has an unapologetic vision of what it wants to be, if you like games that don't hold your hand, then I'd be hard pressed to recommend a more compelling game. One of the few sincerely "hidden" gems I've played in a long, long time. And none of this is accounting for the completely free DLC expansion that essentially acts as a sequel campaign.

name: HEXCRAFT: Harlequin Fair
review: One of the best low-budget games on steam as far as I'm concerned. As a fan of Oleander Garden's other games: The Pagan trilogy, and Hexcraft: Eventide Sigil, I can say with certainty that this is exactly what I expected in the best ways. It feels a bit jank, the graphics are muddy and dated, and it is very frustrating dying with very little warning over and over. It's part of the experience, however. The game is one big immersive-sim-esque puzzle about working out how to get anything done. Leave your morals at the door, rob some people, and acquire black metal shirts to fight off the horrors lurking in dungeons hidden behind shopping malls and alleyways. It's a lo-fi game for sure, but the experience is well worth the 13 dollars.

name: HEXCRAFT: Harlequin Fair
review: I don't really know what I'm doing a lot of the time, and do find myself getting frustrated at various things in Harlequin Fair but the atmosphere and world are so good. Exploring the maps and finding new stuff in corners I swear I've been through before is so thrilling, and the amount of content swimming around in here is so interesting. I am so interested in seeing what else I can find while meandering through Old Toronto.

name: HEXCRAFT: Harlequin Fair
review: Feels like a real interesting way to approach the mystery genre--having the play attempt to interface with this impenetrable space, seemingly against the will of the game. Harlequin Fair promotes this kind of obsessive digging. I love it.

name: HEXCRAFT: Harlequin Fair
review:
Immersively simulate a nightmare. I instigated a gang war in Chinatown so I could plunder tarot cards from those caught in the crossfire. The victims had recited poetry to me moments before. They knew me by name. 

Oleander Garden’s work is consistently haunting, enigmatic, beautiful, infuriating. It’s genuinely fun to bang my head against its walls in an attempt to uncover its secrets. I loved this game. 

Given how unfairly obscure it is, you’re lucky enough to have stumbled upon this page, so don’t let the opportunity go to waste. Buy it already.

name: HEXCRAFT: Harlequin Fair
review:
A great new entry into my favorite subgenre: Immersive Sims That Hate You. Haunting, unapproachable, and chaotic. Felt like an out of control dream. I hate when great games end up underplayed, but something about a system this cryptic existing on the Internet with minimal discussion or explanation around it feels special. I'm happy at least that mystery can still exist in spaces like this.

Ultimately: This rules, buy it. Queer open world games haven't been this good since Barbie Horse Adventures.

name: HEXCRAFT: Harlequin Fair
review:
Dreams and nightmares exist within the illusion of a city. Step on stage and fulfill your role.

HEXCRAFT: Harlequin Fair is an immersive sim, but not quite the same as a Fallout or other such titles. In many ways, this game shows a future where the design principles of the 90s were never abandoned and continued to their inevitable conclusions. I would recommend this game if you're looking for something completely unique and unsettling. Go in with as little information as possible, and maybe you'll understand too.

name: HEXCRAFT: Harlequin Fair
review:
This is an excellent simulation game which really manages to capture the feeling of exploring a game which although unfamiliar at first has a lot of deep simulation going on under the hood. This makes the game feel like a truly alive world where you are rewarded for experimentation once you familiarize yourself with its systems.

The game is also really gorgeous with a strong aesthetic and atmosphere which helps you get lost within it. Definitely recommend.

name: HEXCRAFT: Harlequin Fair
review: The most immersive simulationy imsim released in the past decade at least. Beautifully esoteric and esoterically beautiful. There's cute girls in it and one time I summoned a spooky gremlin and it caused a mall to get wiped out.

name: HEXCRAFT: Harlequin Fair
review: Strange and captivating.. I've been thinking about this game a lot since I bought it/first played it. It's really calling me back. Highly recommend. The atmosphere is top notch, and the mystery is extremely enticing!

name: HEXCRAFT: Harlequin Fair
review:
A great immersive sim filled to the brim with incredible esoteric systems, complimented by a delightfully cryptic world with dozens of different characters.
You can shoot up a mall, or have a zombie do it for you, if an angry character looking to take a relic from someone else doesn't start shooting first. You can brew wild concoctions and fight the chinese triad while dressed as a samurai. You can wrack your brain over where the next obscure puzzle piece fits, or what character has it.
If you love immersive sims and system-driven gameplay, please play Harlequin Fair

name: HEXCRAFT: Harlequin Fair
review:
Great immersive sim, with some world-puzzle elements. All of its characters feel like they interact with each other, are aware of each other. There are a lot of neat ways to navigate the world, and potentially some interesting ways to deal with the game's situations.

I will say that some of its features feature slightly less than I would have liked - the consequence system, for example, is pretty insignificant if you go for certain objectives that require killing characters. Characters also don't really interact with your actions as if you're a real player. I wish that if this were the intended concept that more of the game was built around it, though I likely missed mechanics in that area due to my rush for progress. Maybe this was an intended theme!

This is a minor issue in an excellent game with some really great prose, interesting locations and immaculate vibes. Also, this is a severely, severely transgender game in unsubtle ways - for one, the first item I collected from my storage was estradiol. Hell yeah.

review: In a word, cryptic. The game will explain nothing, write nothing down for you, and not direct you anywhere in particular. And, with how niche this game is, you're not exactly going to have much luck finding a guide. It's a fascinating game, but it can be daunting to play.

name: HEXCRAFT: Harlequin Fair
review:
Very fun im-sim like game. The basic systems (stats, spells, equipment, AI, etc.) are relatively simple, but they come together in a very satisfying way, leading to an illusion of complexity (but in a good way). Casually, there's a fair amount of fun in exploring both the zones and the systems, giving you a lot of small 'ah-ha' or 'oh cool' moments. The emergent interactions in how the AI reacts to certain things - and especially how they can be manipulated in certain ways - is really enjoyable to watch and play with, and can lead to some fun discoveries itself.

The story is... well I'll be honest, I can't really follow it. I couldn't tell you what that means for the quality of the story, it just flat out escapes me. The way it reads, it's either by someone who used hallucinogens at least occasionally as inspiration, or someone with an EXTREMELY good grasp on English and poetry, possibly both. The prose is beautiful, but extremely strange and heavily influenced by pagan and occult views - and from a much farther delve into them than I ever got during my time in those circles.

Fortunately, you don't have to wrap yourself up in the story to enjoy the game. Controls are very tight, systems generally are generally fairly straightforward, RNG is constrained to how NPCs roam and not things like combat, hitboxes are mostly accurate (and usually inaccurate to your benefit more often than not). The zones aren't so massive as to be troublesome, but they're a good size and have some bits worth exploring for.

Casually, I got my first ending in a bit over 3 hours - 3.3 on Steam's playtime. Took me a while after to get the second (of two I'm fairly sure), and I haven't even touched the free DLC that's supposed to be a sort of NG+. I did, however, start speedrunning it, and it's been a blast routing and rerouting as I learned new stuff and got more consistent at the route.

Highly recommended to check out if you like im-sims, but definitely prefer to go at it blind if possible. A lot of the fun for a casual player will come from discovery and exploration. Not everyone likes getting killed by triad over and over while trying to go fast like I do. 

Oh and finally, trans right.

name: HEXCRAFT: Harlequin Fair
review: Perfectly simulates the experience of being a transgender witch girl wandering around at 3 in the morning looking for your girlfriend(?), 10/10!

name: HEXCRAFT: Harlequin Fair
review:
Sadly, for everything I love about the game, there is a problem I fail to tolerate:
[list]
[*]It's cool that the game doesn't hold your hand, but also, it completely fails to articulate the main goal.
[*]There is an interesting simulated world here, but the actual gameplay is extremely unpolished.
[*]Maps are dripping with atmosphere, but running back and forth to your apartment for saving quickly becomes a chore.
[*]The game gives you a lot of items to experiment with, but everything is vague and the inventory is insultingly tight.
[/list]

I liked the demo a lot, so I hate to give a thumbs down, but the full game suffers from its problems just way too much. Despite all of this, I think I'm looking forward to check out the developer's future projects (if there will be any), because I can't deny there is something intriguing about their games.

name: HEXCRAFT: Harlequin Fair
review:
A very confusing one to review. I liked playing it a lot. I was fortunate enough to play the demo and it assured me I would. But when I boil things down, a lot of flaws got in the way of me enjoying it much more.

- I was enchanted by the spellbook--you unlock spells throughout the game and can use it to mess with the environment. By the time I unlocked any spells I was far and beyond needing any of the sandboxy ones like summoning neutral hostiles to attack people. I couldn't conjure a reason to ever use spells to take anything down when I was never punished for using a gun instead.
- No holds barred, the atmosphere is amazing. It's a creepy game but the bottom line is that it's a survival horror more in practice than concept--it's got no jumpscares and still got me pretty good naturally, plus the style is extremely unique in how crisp and muddy everything looks.
- I actually thoroughly enjoyed being aimless without instruction. I always knew there was something to do somewhere, but that was ruined twice at the end of the game. Once when the item I was looking for was in a part of the map I had already searched thrice, every time the place was empty but I'm guessing I progressed enough to finally spawn something there. The other time I actually had to flat out look up the location of the final item I needed to finish the game because I passed by the not-obvious-enough clue a hundred times, and that proved to be its own herculean task--the only true guide to this game turned out to be a speedrun.
- The combat is the worst part of the game handily. The atmosphere is immaculate and there's all these gadgets you can use to your advantage, and yet every encounter boils down to you and your opponent standing still and shooting at each other. They always stand still, I try to dodge but most of the time I get hit anyway because I'm trying to dodge bullets. When I'm not dodging bullets I'm running away to hopefully heal back up. Everything contributes to an eerie, immersive, genuine experience and then the clunky rigid combat pulls me out of it every time.
- I was pretty disappointed to eventually learn that NPCs [spoiler]die forever, mostly because anything they add to the world is extremely fleeting, they inevitably die during your effort to progress. Their place in the world is the most interesting part of the game, and while it's only natural they'd die permanently, they were gone before I truly knew what I had, and I found it tacky that they're usually replaced with nondescript neutral enemies. I was unable to truly enjoy their mechanics, but at the same time, they were able to give me directions to locked map areas early, which on the surface seems like a neat way to progress freely but led to me progressing strangely, like unlocking one of the last spells first.[/spoiler]
- The free DLC was a pretty sweet addition. Despite all my expectations and knowledge, having slight shifts and a remixed area led to a satisfying second go at experiencing the game, even if it was more combat heavy and less ambiance heavy to its detriment.

In the end, despite all my complaints, discovering this game was intoxicating and I realize that all these gripes are more so tied to a first-time experience. I might play this game again immediately, and I will live with these 'flaws' as characteristics of the game. I'm afraid playing it again won't be nearly as good knowing so much more than I started with, but I know for a fact years later I will revisit this game and fall in love again. I enjoyed this more than enough to seek out other work from this dev, who seems to have a bit of a following and a consistent style. But without a doubt, this game will not be for everyone, and I think 2 hours is more than long enough to decide whether you'd want to refund or not. Try this.

name: HEXCRAFT: Harlequin Fair
review:
Oleander Garden's games are interesting because their particular brand of obtuse design rides a fine line between intentionality and facade. I love obtuse games, but there's a level of finesse that needs to be struck for the mechanics to feel engaging and rewarding as you uncover them - if you fumble this, your game risks appearing shallow because those obtuse mechanics don't show themselves and players don't know where to seek them out and experiment with them, so they stick to the safety of what they know (wander around, pick things up, loop through areas to see if anything's changed). To describe Harlequin Fair as "Vampire the Masquerade x KittyHorrorShow x Pathologic" does it a huge disservice because in the end, it's not really like any of those things unless you count the color palette as a direct reference, or the fact that there's plants in it and you can pick them to make potions, idk. Even the immersive inspirations don't chock up because most of them are "guys will shoot other guys if there's shooting happening" and "you can place an explosive and detonate it later." In reality, it's more like if Blood came out a few years later and someone made a mod for it that allows you to talk to some of the monsters and they say poetic things like "is hell hereditary" and then tell you where you might be able to find someone to shoot in order to steal their tarot card.

Oleander's games appear - and claim - to be deeper than the player is capable of knowing at first glance, or second or third, but (in my experience) they also don't take bold enough steps to provoke players into looking further than their initial reactions. This is the sad reality of being an indie artist, especially when making experimental work; the simple truth is that the average player WON'T look deeper unless provoked to. There are layers here, but either too many to understand *why* we should be playing the game in the first place, or too few to back up the promises made in the game's storefront descriptions. What I find in the core of Harlequin Fair is a game where you wander around looking for floating PNGs until you get bored and see what can be accomplished through violence. Turns out that violence IS the answer, but I still don't know what question it's answering - to continue the vague gameplay loop for a nondescript narrative? To test out the immersive systems that just kind of happen without my knowledge? To find items until the game gives me a win screen? I've had the same problem with a few other famous examples already, if the game doesn't "get good until hour 30," and hour 30 is just more of the same, then why am I spending my time on it?

I hate that I've become jaded towards this sort of thing, given my excitement for this game specifically, and my proclivities towards punishing immersive experiences. Blame it on having a full-time job and a crisis of self? Oleander, if you're reading this, I apologize for disliking your piece of art that was tailor-made for me. I'm still keeping my eye on your portfolio and I cannot wait to see how you grow as an artist.

I'll be up front and honest here. Had the game been any bit more expensive than it is. This would have been a negative review. Treat this as a grey/neutral review if you like.

The only thing that's really fun here is getting to full power and being able to fly or combo with the tumbleweed, and it time gates you for as long as it can from getting to that one juicy bit of fun.[spoiler]The secret bonus map is also a huge let down, its huge for no real reason. There's no real secrets in it, and the boss is 10x the health of the normal map, making it take ages to drop if you're the only one not afk, and it largely only drops the same cards as the normal ones just a little stronger, with the exception of two new abilities which really serve no purpose since there's no content past this. I guess you could use them to flex on new players? I don't really see the point personally.[/spoiler]

I wont complain too much more here. Game can still be beat in a day even with the time wasting. It's an OK distraction to break up a routine and thankfully not very expensive.

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
On the surface, this game appears to be shovelware. Half-finished collision boxes and clipart menu graphics leave a bad first impression, but play into it a bit, and at its core are a collection of solid and well thought-out physics puzzles.

My main problem with the game is that the physics are not consistent, which can be a severe detriment to a puzzle game (See Bad Rats), but here it doesn't seem to be a problem at all unless you're going for the "Your bridge broke but you still made it" achievement.

I was surprised by the creativity of the puzzles. The first bridge that gives you a "Checkpoint", or a location your vehicles must drive through before reaching the goal, places the checkpoint almost directly below the starting location, forcing you to think up some insane motocross ramp to reach it safely. And it only gets wackier from there.

Overall a thoroughly enjoyable puzzle game, if you like that sort of thing, with only a few problems that almost never seem to get in the way of gameplay.

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
This game doesn't revolutionize anything, it's the standard bridge builder. But it's a nice game! I've seen some other reviews talking about bugged physics but I just finished every level and nothing weird ever happened.

The definitely-made-for-mobile interface can be a turn off for some people, but it does its job very well, it's not a poor port. The fact that using the right mouse click is very useful goes to prove that, in my opinion.

Some levels have a 'crazy bridge' sign, meaning you'll need to build some outrageous bridge to beat that level. These are very fun, of course, but the construction method start to feel weak in these levels. Feels like you should have more control over the building, like defining exactly what would be solid and what wouldn't etc (these things are automatically defined depending on the angle and adjacent pieces).

While I do recommend this game, judging by the fact that it isn't anything new and it's fairly short, I'd say wait for a sale or something. At full price I'm not so sure if it's worth it.

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
Bridge Constructor surprised me, it sure looks very casual and is as easy to get into as you'd expect. For veterans of the genre who played some of the old hardcore bridge building games though, it not only has a nostalgic effect, but also kinda kicks yer butt in places if you get too cocky. If you have played bridge building games before, you will have an easy and reasonably fun time flying through the first playthrough of the main campaign, which only took me some 2.5 hours. 

The main campaign maps then open up for testing your bridges using the "tank truck", which makes things pretty tough and you have to solve levels differently (with some more budget) compared to your first playthrough! In the Expansion/DLC part of the game, you find some rather different and creative levels that at least to some degree challenge your engrained senses for the rules.

So while the levels in the first run through the main campaign become very much "seen that before", especially if you're playing them quickly, I am still positively surprised by the game overall. If you like games like this and haven't played any in a longer time, give it a go! If you've played them all, then wait for a sale.

Note: The game's difficulty often doesn't leave much room for errors or poor decision-making. Despite it's cutesy appearance, the game would potentially be frustrating for younger kids without guidance.

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
I've played a few bridge construction versions over the years. Compared to the previous one I played, this one added some features and took some others away. New to me were concrete, slope mania, and tanker trucks. Gone were two-way traffic, moving bridges, and the ability to adjust the 3rd dimension (bridge width). Overall this version results in a polishes experience that gets it just right. Sometimes it's hard to build a nice bridge, sometimes you can get away with crazy cheap designs. I enjoy it quite a bit.

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
Bridge Constructor is a nice and simple way to waste some time. The story in the game is very straightforward. There has been a devastating earthquake that has destroyed the bridges on the islands of Camatuga and the player is in charge of their rebuilding. The problem for the player is that the available budget for the bridge constructions is very tight, so it is not possible to make too advanced solutions or to use the more expensive materials as much as would be ideal. After building the bridges, they have to be tested by letting some cars and/or trucks cross and if the bridge collapses a rethink of the design is needed. When all bridges on Camatuga are functioning for trucks, the game opens up for tests using heavy tanker trucks on the newly constructed bridges, with an often sad and explosive result prompting a redesign of the bridges.

Sometimes the statics of the bridge constructions seem a bit off with collapsing at unexpected places, but usually some engineering common sense and experimenting will solve the problems. It would have been nice to be able to play some of the levels with unlimited budget in order to test some truly exotic bridge designs, but the game is good enough as it is.

This is not a game to spend full evenings with because of its repetitive nature, but it is a relaxing game to play for a couple of minutes now and then. Quite a bit like sudoku.

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
Warning to potential buyers: This game has some bridges that won't be solveable with the only structures the game shows you, you'll have to make some weird sh** up and I know this because I had to look up the answer after failing some of the bridges over and over. The game does little to show you which structures you should be thinking about (other than triangles), and often you might not really see if something is working that 10% better than your other builds, and that's often a crucial difference. It also doesn't really explain what materials might fare better beyond the absolute basics of (steel is stronger than wood), and it would've been awesome if they did more to show how the rope can help to support tight budget bridges by connecting along the underside of your bridge from end to end. I only know it distributes weight better this way because some walkthroughs used it. Other materials can be a bit fiddly with load distribution depending on how you've arranged them.
I have played through a bunch of this game and BC: Medieval and while I like both just for the physics puzzle challenge, I find the Medieval one's solutions end up making more sense than this one. It still barely guides you but at least those structures kind of make sense.
In this version you can build diagonal roads (which might be the only way to solve some of these bridges, it's certainly the only way I could find for some) and they end up letting you hold them up with ropes from nonsense structures on the sides because the stone pillars are too expensive or not available.
I still recommend it because I've enjoyed it either way, the physics are simple but pretty consistent, and it's a good little brain work out, but if you want a well-paced puzzle game with some actual help on what to do, this game fails a little on that front. I personally looked up a few answers and still enjoyed trying to figure the level out before I gave up.

review:
Edit2: Some months later, they fixed the achievements. Thumb is up again.
Edit: They added broken achievements to Steam (but not to the game code, that's why they are unachievable), so until this is fixed, here's a THUMB DOWN.

Bridge Constructor is casual gaming at its best, I enjoyed it alot. You can play it in small doses, but the fun is there if you play it for hours, too. It's not a hectic game, you construct your bridge and press a button to see how it does when cars/trucks/tank trucks drive over it. Watching the physics simulation can be fun in itself, especially when the chaos of the collapse brings unexpected results. If the vehicles reach the goal even though your bridge is collapsing behind them, that earns you an achievement aptly named "throwaway bridge" and I got a broad smile when that happened the first time.

If you want to go for high scores (build using less materials, in perfection often resulting in a "throwaway bridge" situation) or compare your score on leaderboards, this game has that, too. It encourages experimentation with the construction options you have. And getting the achievements is also fun and 100%ing is possible with some effort but without pain.

The "sandboxy" character of the game shines when you unexpectedly finish a level because some weird accident happens and the truck gets thrown to the goal while your bridge collapses, or the explosion with the fuel tank truck happens in just the right way to propel its two parts across.

For the later levels that are tricky and require some experimentation, I found it enjoyable to watch other people's solutions on youtube, often even after I solved that level myself.

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
Never trust a bridge you find in Camatuga.  Go well out of your way to find a ferry, or go all the way around the river to get to the other side, but don't go across a bridge!

Bridge Constructor is a game where you build bridges in the fictional lands of Camatuga.  Your budget, and sometimes your available supplies (wood, steel, concrete, and rope) are limited, which means that sometimes you have to get creative.  But beyond that, it's all about figuring out how you can build a bridge, on budget, that will carry across cars, then medium trucks, then tanker-trailers.

While it may sound like a fairly straightforward physics building game, the wacky bridges you'll often wind up with and the explosions of wood and steel when things don't quite work out, make it hilarious.  The game is both a lot of fun by yourself, but also a lot of fun with friends, especially towards the more difficult later levels, where you can pass around the mouse and try your own approaches to building spans on low budgets.  Inevitably it will take a lot of tries to get it right, and laughter will ensue as unlikely bridges collapse - or occasionally prove just good enough to support the load, despite their "I'd never drive across that in real life" appearance.

Among the Bridge Constructor series, I'd put this one and Medieval at pretty much equal for gameplay, as Medieval introduces some interesting new elements, but the three vehicles types per bridge extends the challenge, in a good way, of each bridge here (while not preventing you from moving to the next one as long as you have cars getting across).  However, this iteration gave me much more playtime before I'd completed most of the game, and they're the same price, so my recommendation would be to get Bridge Constructor, have a blast, and if you're still wanting more, picking up Medieval after that.

I found this game sitting in my Humble Bundle library and I have no idea when I got it. It might have been a gift. I decided to give it a try and it is ... okay. I guess. There is two serious problems, however. 

Firstly, the physics is wonky. I created a bridge which held up for the most part, but failed close to the end. I went to reinforce the part that failed, but I needed to know exactly what part failed first. When I reran the trucks across the bridge a second time, it worked perfectly. I then noticed this happening a lot. You could rerun a bridge if it barely failed over and over again and sometimes you would get lucky. 

Secondly, there are better bridge builder games out there. Poly Bridge, for example. That game has a better physics and more variation in the puzzles. 

I would only pick it up if you can get it on sale. Even then, I would recommend looking at the competition first.

I am the Michael Bay of civil engineering, my bridges most of the time collapse in a spectacular fashion sending cars and trucks to their doom in the precipice below. Cables snap, supports break and tank trucks explode mid air. My creations are a sight to behold and will probably give any real civil engineer recurring nightmares but I do love them like a proud father holding up a deformed baby, "he is a special one" I say as the midwife gives me a mournful look. My favorite bridge was actually a success, at least according to the game, as I sent trucks down that bridge, the first support glowed an ominous red, when they cleared the midpoint of the bridge that support collapsed starting a slow downward movement of the left side of the bridge, that was okay though because just as the whole thing collapsed by first truck cleared the bridge and the second one was tumbling onto the road on the right side of the bridge. A spectacular success!!!

On a serious note, it's not a bad game but the physics don't feel right and the resulting bridges that succeed according to the game look like abominations you will never see in real life more often than not. Get it on a sale but don't expect realistic physics.

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
I really don't want to give this a solid "recommended" rating, but there's no middleground with these reviews. It's not so bad that it's outright unplayable and there's certainly a degree of fun to it, but it's hardly the quirky bridge puzzler I was hoping for.

As so many others have stated, the biggest issue going for the game is that the physics are inconsistant. The same bridge will not fail the same way twice a lot of the time, sometimes even netting you a win for the level. It's one thing to say your puzzle game has multiple solutions, and another to say it's down to luck. This is compounded by the fact that quite often the successful bridges don't look like they SHOULD work, this is especially true in later levels.

I'd say the first half of the game is fairly fun and thought-provoking, while the later half, especially once you add in the Tank Trucks, is more an exercise in frustration. You might try a bridge that should work but cannot find out why it doesn't, look up a guide and discover you were basically doing it right except one piece of wood was in the wrong spot. That doesn't feel rewarding at all.

Speaking of unrewarding, some of the bridges are almost exactly the same as earlier ones, except suddenly you only have wood, or have less money. So the bridge that barely held together before is suddenly even less practical and the solutions you find in guides are so far removed from common sense that it's a wonder someone found them. When they work, of course, since the guide solutions in my experience only work about 70% of the time. Again, it boils down to the inconsistancy of the game's physics.

That makes the experience unique, I suppose.

I did notice that certain ideas or solutions seem to repeat themselves a lot. Such as placing a short vertical piece of wood/steel under the connect points and then stringing along wire parallel to the bridge. It's amazing how often that works cause in reality it sure as hell wouldn't. I think that's part of why the puzzles don't feel like proper puzzles, cause the solutions don't really come from a logical point of view, but throwing crap together and seeing what holds on long enough for the vehicle to make it across.

I realize this all sounds fairly negative, despite the fact I'm giving it a Thumbs Up. Essentially I'm saying the game is not that great but if you got it on sale it might be a fun way to kill some time. And maybe a few brain cells after beating your head against the wall at some point.

name: Bridge Constructor

Admittedly, there's different distances, and different anchor points, but there's not the variety that BC: Portal has early in the game. Also, the physics are questionable in places.  I'm not an expert, but I have made bridges that are very very wobbly and look to defy gravity.

Given that BC: Portal is only a few pennies more expensive, I'd recommend getting that.  This, in the current year, is only really worth picking up if you have already played through BC: Portal and have a real distinct itch to do more.

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
[h1]Overview:[/h1]
'[i]Bridge Constructor[/i]' is one of many construction games where the player must build a bridge sturdy enough to carry a variety of vehicles from one side to the other while keeping within budget and without the bridge breaking. You play as a bridge engineer as you build your way through 5 islands all of which are in desperate need of bridges because for some reason roads are paved but blueprints for bridges are left unaddressed. You go from building simple and straightforward bridges to gigantic monstrosities that more or less resemble roller coasters than an actual bridge. Although the idea sounds fun enough, it soon becomes more stressful and frustrating than fun and amusing and that is due to a multitude of reasons.

[h1]Gameplay:[/h1]
Honestly these types of games aren't my cup of tea so take this review with a grain of salt. It might not be as bad as I portray it to be as I myself am not a physics major but I think many agree that the physics in the game alone are inconsistent and not very good. Although it didn't take me extremely long to understand how the mechanics worked, it still didn't change the fact that the things you have to build in this game are absurdly ridiculous. The window of constructing bridges in later levels become so small and tight that it feels like there are only one or two possible solutions and that has a lot to do with the budget feature in the game. It's like every island you're contracted to had budget cuts so severe that I could've sworn I heard the board talking about implementing loot boxes to their marketing campaign to increase revenue.

I can understand giving players a challenge by limiting the resources they have to build a bridge, but the bar they set for money conservation is so high that you practically need to make the most flimsy and extremely brittle overpasses that coast on the verge of breaking just because the city said "[i][b]Hey, that's too much money for a bridge that'll keep our citizens safe! Take this instead![/b][/i]". The foundations and materials also range from wood, metal, stone pillars and wires and depending on what location and level you're playing, you're only given specific ones. So at the end of the day, you're not only limited to a strict budget, but also the resources the game provides you with. You don't get to unlock better materials, different foundations or anything that could give the player creative freedom. It's just what the game says you can use which limits potential solutions even more.

Had there been a casual mode where annoying budgets were nonexistent, I feel like the game might actually be fun. But as is, the limited resources the player's given as a whole makes the entire game a frustrating trek because the city just doesn't give a damn how dangerous a bridge is, as long as the vehicles testing it make it across. They don't care if it's next to impossible to build a bridge with a $20,000 budget, just make it happen! I at one point even had a bridge tested and break just to reset it and have it somehow hold together proving that the physics don't even follow their own rules. It becomes less about "[i][b]what awesome bridge can I build?[/b][/i]" and more about "[i][b]what the fuck actually works?[/b][/i]". It's just a game that tries to be like more popular bridge constructor games but fails to do so on more than one account.

[h1]Controls:[/h1]
Besides placing anchors on a grid, there's not much else to talk about in regards to controls as most of it is just observing the finished product.

[h1]Graphics:[/h1]
Not bad but not great either. Resembles other typical bridge constructor games and looks [i]very[/i] mobile-like. That may or may not be a bad thing depending on preference but personally I'm not a fan of them. Unlike the tanker, cars and trucks behave like toys, there's no damage or anything when they fail a bridge test. As for the trailer, that's probably the most fun because when it falls, it explodes! Nevertheless, the graphics are very underwhelming and just makes you feel like you're playing a mobile game.

[h1]Audio:[/h1]
Also not great. The sounds and music work fine enough but nothing stands out or sounds particularly great. The sound effects consist of below average vehicle sounds (some to which abruptly cut off) along with the annoying sound of bridges collapsing that you'll very soon come to hate. The music also gets repetitive and annoying fast but thank god there's a way to mute it because I need peace and quiet while I build the world's ugliest, flimsiest and most dangerous bridge with as little money as possible.

[h1]Conclusion/TL;DR:[/h1]
'[i]Bridge Constructor[/i]' is a physics-based puzzle game with a huge learning curve where you build bridges with specific materials while on a strict budget. The concept has been done more times than needed but the game itself is unfortunately one of the black sheeps of the flock. The budget feature is the main killer for many players (myself included) and cripple almost every potential (and safe) solution most levels could possibly have because the maximum amount of money you're allowed to spend is unrealistically low. This means only very few bridge options work and all fly dangerously close to succumbing which makes some levels almost impossible to beat. It also praises itself for having a cool physics engine but even the engine is inconsistent as some bridges will fail one time but pass the second. Overall, '[i]Bridge Constructor[/i]' tries to be like many of it's brothers and sisters but ended up being the ugly duckling of bridge constructors.

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
Great game. I hope there will be a new version with more details, helping me not only play but also learn more knowledge about bridge construction. I would like to contribute ideas for the next version as follows:

1. There is an additional section for instructions, detailing the types of materials, for example: what type of wood is being used, size (length, width, height), weight/m3, bearing capacity horizontally/vertically etc. similarly have detailed specifications for iron, steel wire, concrete pillars, it would be great to add bamboo or other types of wood.

2. There is information about the weight of the vehicles.

3. There are tools for players to customize:
a) Manually adding new materials and specifications (name, weight, cost, bearing capacity, etc.).

b) Customize new vehicles.

c) Create custom maps.

Players created materials, vehicles, and maps can be shared through the workshop, they are only used for them to explore and challenge each other (in sandbox mode) and will not be scored.

4. There are more options for vehicles to cross the bridge when there is an earthquake or high wind (with earthquake levels from 1-7 and wind strength from level 1-12), or multiple vehicles moving on the bridge at the same time. (instead of only 1-2 cars like the current version). At this time, the bridge will shake more violently.

5. Players can optionally display (or pay) to see more detailed information of the bridge such as: weight of the bridge deck after construction is complete, load capacity, vibration resistance , which displays force vectors (as arrows) to see exactly what happens when a vehicle passes.

Overall, I hope the game can be more detailed and realistic, so that it is no longer just a game, but will become a software that construction students can use to learn.

In order for it not to become boring, the game can be divided into many modes, the campaign mode will be the same as it is now, not too difficult to play and should be more interesting in the scenario, the sandbox mode includes the content custom as I stated.

Bridge Constructor is fun, and often challenging. Especially early in the game, if you don't remember anything from having built matchstick bridges and buildings in school 20 years ago. Sorry, was that suspiciously specific?

Once you figure out the physics and how they play, things get more predictable ... but that's where the budgetary challenges and the need to support multiple vehicle types come in.

Oh, and don't forget all the personal trauma you'll experience with the murders of innocent car and truck drivers on your hands dying on bridges too feeble for their traversal. What could be more fun?

Well-designed, consistent mechanics in an enjoyable leisurely atmosphere. I definitely recommend giving this one a go.

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
I played through the first island and there's a lot of things in this game that are frustrating for no reason or could be easily improved. 

1) There's no consistency on the availability of building materials. You unlock steel cables, but you can't use them on this level... or this one! Ha ha! Even the final bridge of the first island was wood only. Complete BS.

2) The UI is very clunky. You click on the button for wood or steel and half the time it plants a node right next to the button and you have to pan the view around to delete it. Frustrating.

3) All stress is one color, there's no visual differentiation between compression or tension. I played a bridge builder 20 years ago that had compression as blue and tension as red. The engine obviously knows which is which but the developer is just being lazy.

4) There's no drag and drop for nodes. Other bridge builders will let you drag an existing node to shorten or lengthen supports and it will display the cost difference live. This game makes you rip the node and everything connected to it out. 

5) There's no mouse over text or tool tips for any material. "Ok good job you unlocked steel, but we're not going to tell you how strong it is or how much it costs!"

6) There's levels that are very obviously designed to let you get a specific achievement. "You came in $25k under budget on a 3 section span? Nice job Financial Genius or whatever." Achievements should be created from the game that exists, not drive the design of the game.

7) Finally, you get the whole island completed to 100% and they are like, "Ok now you can have tanker trucks use your bridges!" and all of your progress drops to 83%. Nope, uninstalling forever goodbye.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: Surprisingly good game. I am glad I didn't miss this game, it's a well executed game, and a welcome return to the classic hex turn based games. The fact that I love the Warhammer world certainly helps. I haven't played through the game yet, but I certainly will. But as good as I think this game is it does have some points that need improvement. Save games doesn't follow the storyline. By that I mean that if you save a game, you can't simply press continue and think the game will load up the latest save, and continue from there. No, it will instead reveal *all* chapters to you, and you choose where you want to play from. Nice gesture, but not so much on the first playthrough. All of a sudden you need to pick the chapter you were last time (and it's not certain you'd remember that) moreover it will start at the beginning of said chapter. Probably not what you want. Instead, you need to load the save and the save isn't named the text you gave it, no it's named after the place and time you saved it. Again, this isn't all that clear to a user. No, please fix so that a continue selection means just that, it will continue from whence you saved last. Also, please bring the naming into savegames, I typically (and I know others do too) save a temp file, that I want to use when I *don't* want to store a specific time and place. Seeing that name helps. Also, please don't let users see all the chapters on the first playthrough, it isn't helping the game at all. It breaks the story, it is like a kid winding the movie to the action scenes, skipping the booring (but oh so essential) 'talkie bits' :) But besides that, the game (at least to me) is good and very enjoyable.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
Bought this game with multiplayer being the main reason. Me and my brother are old school WH40k fans and fight a long and endless "tabletop war" between orks (me) and marines (him). So this game was a no brainer to buy when we heard about it.

Being a fan of Final liberation i can say that it is very similar in many aspects and i like it. BUT! This game with it`s cool drawn units has no movement and death animations at all (something that old Final Liberation HAD!), has no decals like craters and burns being left after barrage (something that even Steel Panthers of old 90th had). So i was a bit dissappointed by the high price and the lack of "technology" behind it.

Next, multiplayer - it is PBEM and it has no turn replays! So you have to recall everything that was prior to the current turn and burn your brain imaginating what caused the outcome. Multiplayer feels like being wired in as the last and heavily untested feature. Also we were promissed to have a hotseat mode. There is no hotseat in the game right now.

I would recommend this game to WH40k fans, but be aware that it has little difference from what you get on the table, except for miniatures producing sounds and fancy shotting effects.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: Love the 40k universe and as much as I loved all the Dawn of War stuff, it's nice to be able to play a turn-based strategy game.  Doesn't exactly mirror the tabletop game rules, but plenty of unit types and  it's quite fun so far.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
http://www.slitherine.com/forum/viewtopic.php?f=235&t=54402

How Disappointing!


So yeah, title pretty much says it all.

Played 40k for over 22 years now and this is just bad. There is no denying it, it feels almost as if it's not even a GW game.

Tool tips are poor at best, even though information is given still not sure whats it's about!

Gretchin with 80% accuracy? really? did you guys even know what a gretchin is like in the game and yet he has a 80% accuracy and gets a range and melee attack? They had blunderbuss's and that 1 attack range or 1 attack melee not both. kinda stupid gretchin fighting as good as rough riders.

Graphics look rather dated.

Unit list? If i have the resource points to buy something why can't i? Why would you limit the number of units a person can have plus combine a resource manager? If i want to swarm with conscripts why can't i? why do i have ot say hey well your useful but since i'm limited on how many units i can your not good enough when there are better.

Mission objects. WTF are they? Was falling back south to the second continent when i had to do a jungle defense. turn 8 i get told defeat. No orcs had made it through my line, they hadn't captured any bases past me and i hadn't pushed forward.

I reload the map and restart it and it DOES NOT give any mission objectives. I click continue when i'm defeat and it prompts me to start from the every 1st mission again. Do you realise how annoying it is to be told your defeated when you have no idea why?

Experience? No idea how this plays out or how to earn it.

Scouting with rough riders or scout tanks is a damn nightmare, haviong fog of war and no option to use move points they haven't been used it just a waste of troops. Don't know how many times a rough rider unit moves forward half way it's possible move and run into orks and then died as sure you only moved half your possible distance but now you can't move anymore. This mechanic makes scouting pointless might as well turn fog of war off.

why no tool tips when you hover over orders, you realise how much hassle it was to work out how to repair units?

Support units, how exactly do they work? as this is no mechanic i know of in 40k.

Army size small.

Map size tiny to what i thought they would be.

Yet once again i get lured into a death trap of a game due to the GW symbol and to think i was giving this a go due to Blood Bowl 2 being put back. Damn you BB.

Let just say friends of mine have asked for a review on Monday on what i think and so far i'm giving this a 3/10.

sure it's a turn based game and i like that thing, but it plays nothing like 40k from which it's drawn.

Now as for the limited Armies to pick from, can't say how Highly Disappointed i am!!!!!!

Orks and Imp Guard really? Now i've read this forum and i see dev saying hey this is all the IP rights we have. Well guess what either your screwing us or GW is as i know Black Templars and Salamanders codex 1st came out with the release of Armaggedon. Eldar craftworlds also came out, i still have the Armaggedon Codex in my games room if you want to try dispute that one with me.

Plus there was also chaos, so really this is a rather poor pathetic attempt to suck money out of GW fans with a sub standand game that has only the title and unit names are from the 40k GW universe that this is suppose to be planned from.

Now how exactly does 1 get a refund since this is nothing like what i expected to get when i paid my hard earned $30US for.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
This game is brilliant, it is a very solid wargame in the warhammer 40k universite, if you are tired of ww2 wargames or like the warhammer universe you should definately love it. It is very close to panzer general, but the sci-fi twist really makes it an outstanding and novative game. 
As far as content goes i have played ten hours and i am not even done with the campaign, if you add to this a level editor and some built in scenario and you could probably add a zero to this playing time if you get hooked. If this game is followed and they add some new races\campaign this will be the closest you will ever get to the tabletop game.

To list really quick (and just after 10 hours of gameplay) the + and - of this game i would say:

-Good campaign with interesting scenarios, and some cinematic moment, you will surprise yourself when you send three tanks to save this salamander scout just because you have it since the first mission and do not want it to die, alas surrounded by ork mobs this poor scout have known a dire fate.

-Solid wargame with good mechanics, deep but accessible.

-The w40k universe has really been respected, most units are used the way you would use them in the tabletop game, weapons loadout are almost the same (with some few tweek to fit in a wargame i guess)

-Huge replay value

-A decent Ai that will really test your skills on higher difficulties.

-It is a 2d game, wich i think is the best choice for wargame, you have a much better visibility on the battlefield and it is less tedious to move your units around.

As for the negative sides:

-Well the animation is not that good, but some people might like the minimalistic approach like this minimalistic approach.

-Some sounds effect could be improved, the music is quite good, and you have a different sounds for each weapons wich i find neat, but your units are quite, some things like different screams when the units die would have make the experience a bit more fun.

-It is a 2d game, you might not like it, if you can't stand 2d game, or are into superfancy video quality that might not be for you.

-I think the price is fair, you get much more content than a lots of AAA game, but it might be too expansive for some people. if you are a fan of dawn of war and was looking for something close to it you might be disappointed,
if you are a fan of the tabletop game, and the various adptation that has been made of it (rites of war and final liberation), or are into wargame and want a change of scenery you might consider it cheap.

I hope this opinion about this game helped you, it is really a good game as long as you know what you are getting into, i have been positively surprised i did not expect this game to be that good, and i really want to see it grow, it deserves it.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
I. Love. This. Game!

The price at release might seem a bit steep, but if you're a 40k fan then it's probably worth it. I found they captured the 40k atmosphere well, the unit diversity is quite frankly incredible, and there's sufficient strategic depth to keep the strategy game fans among us interested. If you're crazy about 40k and strategy games, get it. If not, you could wait for a sale I guess.

For the Emprah!

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
Very happy with this purchase.

Disclaimer: oldf*g, 40k nerd for life, patient. 

This game is the closest experience yet to playing Epic 40k Space Marine board game from the 80s and 90s. In assuming the story of one of the cornerstone campaigns to 40k lore, the Developers bring a folkish charm to wargaming tropes that will assuredly bring a grin to the beard of the most curmudgeonly of nerds.

Interface is very simple, delivering you straight to the hex-based heaven beyond. Options are minimal, would like to run it in a window.  Graphics are indeed *Dun dun DUNNNN* 2D! detailed renders of the various units, and enough Leman Russ variants to fill a page, which if the idea of which leaves you drooling, run don't walk to the buy button.

I haven't tried the scenario editor yet.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: The game is expensive and graphics are just appropriate. But that´s to be expected in this kind of niche game and not something turn/hex based strategy gamers should be surprised by. If you like 40k, the game is a must have and will give you a good experience at batallion level tactical battles. Also, the campaign is immersive and gives you a feel of the Imperium and of the Armageddon campaign. Very cliche, but that´s 40k for you. I´m enjoying it and recommend it, not to speak of the multiplayer possibility via pbem and future mods and dlcs with other races and campaigns.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
Game is exactly as described by the advertising.  A deep, turn-based wargame set in GW's 40K universe. No, it is NOT a direct port of the tabletop game, which seems to be what many were expecting, based on the negative comments here. Yes, it IS a 2D game, which is quite obvious based on the screenshots. There's a lot to enjoy here if you are perceptive and intelligent enough to have realistic expectations. Slitherine has an excellent track record for hex based TBS games and also for the immense amount of post-release support they give them. I have enjoyed playing this one very much and have great expectations for the future supporting releases for the product. The Lordz Game Studio does great justice to the venerable license with their work here. 

All in all a great, deep, and atmospheric INTERPRETATION of the 40K universe. But if you purchase this expecting 40K tabletop on the PC, you'll be sorely disappointed.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: Dave, it is me.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
NOT A LOVE LETTER TO 40K FANS.  Simply put, this game is lazy.  Lore is scarce but accurate.  Art, fair for it's breed.  Gameplay, not even the crudest attempt at innovation has been made - it may in fact be a step or two backwards from other, older hex based games.  The game mechanics and the most basic meaning of some unit stats are left entirely undefined, even in the manual they bothered to slap together, never mind the near total void of mouse over/click tool tips.  Sound and voice work is... ok, uninspired.  Credit for a vast array of unit variations true to GW Forge World.  Not 100% but far closer than you are likely to see anywhere else.

I strongly believe any 40K fan site with a handful of volunteer modders could do a vastly superior job if handed the same license.  A personal love of 40K is the only thing that makes this game remotely interesting.  Absent the license, it is forgetable shovelware.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
I just finished the campaign yesterday.  I was very excited about it when I first saw it.  I would love to say it's a good game, and it has alot of good things (which youtube and steam reviews are posting no small portion of).  I certainly won't say it was a bad game, but without repeating what other people have already said, I have some problems with it because I felt largely disappointed with this game.  Let me begin with its virtues, while trying not to repeat what everyone talks about in depth.

Pros
1) A present plot of sorts taking place during one of the major campaigns in 40k history (I didn't mind the voice acting too much)
2) Nearly all inclusive by way of units (Forge World and Games Workshop both)
3) Almost all Imperial Guard units are Steel Legion themed (some crews are still Cadians)
4) Very classic and well structured game mechanics
5) This game can be played without ANY space marine support (which I largely found inferior to fielding more Imperial Guard hardware).  Finally a game that doesn't cater to GW's "space marines are what it's all about" theme.
6) The weaponry works fairly true to canon and tabletop standards (a Punisher gatling cannon is good against infantry, but does nothing to vehicles).  Basically, if it'd work on the tabletop, it'll work here (and vice versa).

Cons
1) Beyond the dialogues between missions, the plot is absent to the gameplay.  I would have liked some cutscenes (at least a closing one).  Instead, the campaign ended and it just sent me back to the main menu.  Additionally, they added some elements that seemed rather unnecessary (such as the inquisitor showing up and trying to "recruit" you).

2) The forces you can assemble lack a sense of proportion to the realism of the 41st Millenium.  In the final mission, I fielded approximately 10 Reaver titans, 8 Basilisks, 9 Steel Legion infantry units, plus the "command group" of Yarrick, 2 chapter masters, the drill sergeant, and the detachment of Ultramarines.  For easily the later half (if not 2/3 starting in part 2), the titans and basilisks took up the VAST bulk of the fighting with infantry moving up only to capture objectives.  I didn't feel like titans were rare assets, or limited in number available to the war waging across an entire planet.

3) By way of the Factions (Imperium and Orks), the armies/clans within them are almost completely left out.  They mention extensively about how you're part of the Armageddon Steel Legion, but they make no mentions to the Ash Waste Militia, Hive Militia, or even Jungle Fighter regiments that Armageddon maintains in its PDF (the best they get is Hive Gang Militia units, which are hardly regimental strength).  Stormtrooper companies and every Ork clan (like Goffs, Evil Sunz, Snakebites, etc) are completely left out.  Variances between the Space Marine chapters are minor at best.

4) While a hex based game brings back ALOT of good memories for us long termed gamers, for a 2014 game, I expected better graphics, unit models to face all directions (that was mentioned in another review).  Aircraft function like aircraft, not another ground unit who's only unique feature is they can move over impassible terrain like rivers and mountains.  A more prominent role for the VIP's of the campaign (Dante, Yarrick, Ghazghkull, etc).

In short, Warhammer 40k: Armageddon is a nice game, excellent in how it even breaks from the Games Workshop ultimate sanction for space marines and their typical themes of Cadian uniformed guardsmen praising the Angels of Death for saving them.  They incorporated almost everything a 40k player (particularly an Imperial Guard tank player) could want for their army.  The game mechanics can be said to be reasonably good.  However, this game has no feel of being new.  I would have expected to see this game as it stands back in 1995 (give or take), not 2014 (almost 20 years later). It also still needs some work to get completely in tune with the 40k lore (like Imperial Guard regiments, Ork clans, etc).  I was hoping for a GREAT game, but found a decent one instead.  Even with it's 20 year retro and my minor grievances regarding 40k lore to game practicalities, I'd still suggest people have a go at it.
