name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
In short: surprisingly good!

This game is targetted to boths fans of the Panzer commander games and Warhammer 40k players. It uses the engines of Panzer but it is played in the world of 40k. It's a tactical game in a grand campaign through which you go from mission to mission. It's a good plot with quite a few surprise in there.

The good news: the engine is solid, the AI is good, it mounts good offense and counter-attack. The scenarios are well designed and provide a good challenge. It provides overall a good gameplay experience, which is key. Unit variety is incredible (in the hundreds).

The bad news: the units graphics are not well animated (almost no animation) and the soundtrack is minimalist. Titans are too small, they should have been bigger.

This is a must for any wargamer that loves the 40k universe. The sheer joy of finally playing with super-heavy tank and Titans is reward enough.

It's still early but it looks like the community will be active on this one and design other 40k factions and scenarios/campaign to add to the overall gameplay experience and durability of the game.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
The Emperor must be smiling along with many a wargaming grognard who revels in classical strategy board games and similar 1990s video game translations. Warhammer 40,000 Armageddon is everything we love of the Games Workshop universe with over the top imagery and bombastic campy inspiring writing coated in the finest Slitherine sauces of stats and modifiers.

Missions are difficult and require proper use of troops and reinforcement resources along with tight timing and luck. Each individual soldier etc shown represents hundreds of units if not thousands. Truly mass carnage worthy of Warhammer 40,000 Armageddon.

Battles feel like a blood drenched death cloaked Warhammer 40k campaign of purified rage with huge losses by both you and the enemy. Many units have multiple weapon systems which fire at different ranges such as bolt weapons,flamers hitting at closer distances while canons, artillery and lasers fire further. All of this is wonderfully displayed on the interface showing weapon type, range and damage with red meaning the weapon is out of range and green is within a weapon's range adding even more strategic depth to combat as one decides how to best engage the enemy or not . It feels great to know exactly what weapons are firing at any given moment thanks to the great ui. 

No broken up disembodied scenarios here. Everything feels connected like a Dreadnought's crushing fist from its metal mechanical combat structure. A most blissful engaging gaming euphoria.

Graphics are done really well with stunning detail where one can zoom in to indulge in the visceral imagery of units. The user interface is a veritable wealth of information showing things like weapon damage, range, chance of hitting and possible damage, type of weapons carried etc. By clicking on units I was taken to a screen with mesmerizing artwork which displayed all the strengths and gear of the current unit.

On another note it was a pretty cool moment to see in the tutorial mission for Warhammer 40,000 Armageddon the seedier side with the obviously corrupt official making his self aggrandizing speech before the start of the scenario. A shocker to see compared to the strict iron will society of the space marines we mainly see in video games. I always thought Commisars were strict with fanatical military discipline. The voice acting in these scenes are excellent bringing home the over the top campy extremes of the Warhammer 40k universe.

Music and sound effects explode like a doomsday orchestra shaking the senses with sonic devastation across the brutal battlefields.

Warhammer 40,000 Armageddon has been compared to Slitherine's masterpiece Panzer Corps but after playing both one will see they are different beasts with their own unique character.

With the patch update of 2-13-15 we even get to play with the brutish thumping Ogryns while enjoying a ton of new added audio effects such as unit death sounds and more dramatic music at the start of turns, after a victory or defeat.

As of Patch 108 June 2015 am loving the new tutorial tips explaining individual game mechanics as one comes across them. Excellent work and reminds me of reading modifier tables on the fly from table top game manuals while playing seeing what effects what and why. Cheers :)

Warhammer 40,000 Armageddon is a must buy if your into table top gaming and if you loved past games in the series such as Warhammer 40,000 Final Liberation.

Warhammer 40,000 Armageddon a tense turn based strategy wargame from Slitherine whose forte are tabletop style games created for the digital gamer. Buy this strategic masterpiece and Praise the Emperor with every deafening blast of bolter firing gaming session!

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
*** I used a stemkey provided by the developer for review purpose ***

Before jumping into this review I want to make one thing perfectly clear. My review of this game is going to based on what the game actually is and not what I think or feel it should be, unlike so many others on the internet who have been swift in their criticisms mostly for all the wrong reasons. The expectations are naturally high due to the popularity of the Warhammer franchise, but an apple is never going to taste like an orange nor is a hex-based tactical game going to play like an RTS. It seems like everyone is forgetting that the Warhammer 40,000 series began as a board game (similar to games like BattleTech, etc) and was first released on PC as a turn-based strategy wargame in 1998. Since that time, Warhammer has been released in many gaming genres including FPS, Action, and RTS (just to name a few) so perhaps many people simply are not aware of its origins. Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon is intended to breath life into a genre that has been increasingly neglected and offer its fans a different flavor of gaming.

Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon is a solid turn-based strategy game that feels very similar to Panzer Corps, which is another game made by the same publisher. Many have said that it is simply the same game but skinned over with the Warhammer brand, but its not. The user interface is exactly the same and it does use the same game engine but that's where the similarities end. Armageddon features very detailed 3D graphics, a completely different Ai since most combat is ranged, a unique and extensive upgrade system for units, and most importantly, a storyline which plays out over the course of 30 missions with varying objectives. So, while it is based on the Panzer Corps concept, it is not the same game.

This game includes over 300 variations of Warhammer units that will surely keep most fans happy. Throughout the campaign players get to keep their core units and upgrades as long as they survive each mission. The maps are intricately designed and offer a variety of terrains that influence combat modifiers. The gameplay is very simplistic and accommodating for most any player. The developers are still active in improving the game and have recently released a free DLC that includes 5 new missions. Several patches have been released already to improved game balancing and a few other issues while adding some new things, which leaves me optimistic about future patches. This game also features a multiplayer feature that gives it extra replay value.

However, like most games, it does have its downfalls as well. Due to its recent release and its infamous name, this game currently carries a hefty price tag considering its content and lack of DLC packages. Since it was intended to be suitable for all types of players, it falls short in truly challenging experienced wargame fans. some of the components are vague at best and fall short of their intended purpose. Glory points are a good example of this. Players receive them, but what are they actually used for? The user interface could use a bit more tweaking as well but it is functional in its current state. Many of the units are strikingly similar and fail to deliver a truly diverse array of combat options.

The Good:

=======

+  Highly detailed units unique to Warhammer
+  Virtually bug-free
+  Solid campaigns with a good storyline
+  Multiplayer for added replay value
+  Steam Trading Cards
+  Intelligent AI
+  Unit upgrade system
+  Level editor included
+  Active developers
+  Free DLC adding extra gameplay

The Bad:

======

+  Falls short of challenging experienced genre veterans
+  Lack of unit diversity
+  User Interface needs improved still
+  Some elements are still vague or pointless
+  Expensive

Final Thoughts:

===========

Overall, this is a fun game and fans of turn-based strategy games or the Warhammer universe in general will enjoy this game. Initial reviews were mixed for a variety of reasons, mostly due to the Warhammer name without an RTS style, but many improvements have been made since the game's initial release and more are in development. While its current base price will scare many away, not picking this game up on sale would be a mistake.

[i]If you liked this review or want to see more recommended games, be sure to follow our curator group: [url=http://steamcommunity.com/groups/Original_Curators_Group#curation]Follow Original Curator Group[/url][/i]

review:
I am a fan of the WH 40 k universe as i am a fan of turn based hex field strategy games.
So i was exited to hear that a game bring both together, is under production .

I bought the game the first week it was released.
Very fast i became frustrated playing the game and i was very dissapointed.
The product not at all fits to what i demand for my hard earned money.
The graphics basicly is the same from fantasy general made in hmmm 1996 0r 97.
Not the slitest improvements.
Well in 2015 it looks simply ugly.
And the game mechanics are crude and primitive.
The software developers who made this have absolutely no idea what players want , why players play a game.
The comfort of controlling was very poor and shows a lack  of ideas or simply no interest.
Well, at one point it started when i got no additional ressources to purchase or even to repair units.
Some maps later i got ressources and could repair again .
The manual and game says NOTHING about this.
In some forum i read about some " batallion value " if you batallion value is to high , you get no further ressources until later in the campaign the Bat value raises above your current troops value.
The game says nothing about this and additional you dont know when your value is to high.
Buy a new tank can make that you get ZERO ressources for the next two maps and yeah you can not repair anything.
This is really annoying and one example for the bad performance by the software developers.

Finally i am very dissapointed that i wasted my hard earned money for this poor and uninspired game.
Dont buy it !

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: This is quite simply one of the worst games I've ever played and I'm disgusted that I didn't do more research prior to buying it.  I'm sure like most, I was excited that there was a new Warhammer 40K title out and rushed to buy.  Wow, what a mistake.  The graphics are simplistic beyond compare to any other 40K title.  In fact, I'd still be disappointed by the graphics and basic nature of the game if this was on my mobile phone, not to mention my PC.  What a complete waste of $30.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
Armageddon is probably the most faithful transition of the tabletop game to the computer to date. It feels very much like the tabletop game while being just differnet enough and the unit selection is great. The game itself I highly recomend. There are quite a few mission where I really had to think in order to win, which is exactly what a stratagy game needs to do.

HOWEVER, the game is very buggy. :( I've put in about 20-30 hours of gameplay and it's crashed my computer 4 times and crashed the game 11. (It does autosave every turn so at least you don't lose a ton of play time.) Also there is a bug that if you restart a level while on an opponant's turn you can't finish you deploy step and start the mission over. 

Overall, I did still have fun and I don't regret my purchase. But the high level of annoiance with the bugs takes what I feel should be something like a 8.5-9/10 to a 6/10. Tenetive recomendation for now, but once they fix the bugs (if the do) then you should get it for sure.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
A fun game to be sure, this title is a prime offering for individuals that want to dip their toes into TBS fare.  While not worth the outrageous offering price of 40 dollars, the game is worth purchasing if you can find the game on a hefty Steam sale.  Unfortunately, the game lacks the deep, rich tactical combat that it purports to have and, instead, exists as an annotated view of the the tactical/wargamer genre.  Warhammer 40K fans will be delighted at the amount of units but most individuals would probably be better served purchasing the game on the iPad.  The game runs just as well, controls just as fluidly, costs less and its difficulty level better matches a portable/mobility environment.

In fact, had the game not shipped on iOS almost a full year after I purchased it on Steam, I'd never have bought it for PC.  Weapons are said to affect different units in different ways but this is wholly untrue most of the time.  What's truly disappointing is paying 40 dollars for this game, which lacks any appreciable cut scenes, fleshed out story, unit animations, complex combat system or pervasive mulitplayer/modding community and yet experiencing Slitherine's absolute gall at asking for 20 dollars a scant year later for what amounts to a small expansion.  Seriously? You just charged us the price of a triple A game, despite obviously providing a much simpler experience and you can't give us some goodies and support before you hit us up again for money? 

Worth a look.

7/10.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
If you love Warhammer 40,000 you might like this game.

This game has a lot of missions, a lot of units, and a map editor.

There ARE some glaring issues with this game, most notably the lack of movement animations for any units.  The missions are also repetitive and the use of "victory hexes" gets old after the first act.  There are also only three factions: Imperium, Space Marine, and Ork.  The game IS about the Second War for Armagedon (a major conflict in the lore), but I hope they make a sequel that adds the rest of the factions on a less centered story.

I am recommending this game only for hardcore fans of WH40K, if you aren't then skip it until the price goes WAY down.

Maybe the game feels low budget, maybe the AI isn't good, and/or maybe the secret sauce just isn't here and the game is no fun for me.  I'm not sure, so I requested a refund.

:-/

P.S. I absolutely loved "Final Liberation" as a kid.  If you want a compelling turn-based strategy game set in the 40k universe, that's your game.  You will not be disappointed by it, but you will be disappointed that they never made a sequel.

One thing that I was surprised wasnt in the game was a map editor or skirmish mode, something that I think would add a ton more replay value to the game. name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
If you played and loved Final Liberation then this is the game for you. While it doesn't possess the awesome FMV cut-scenes of its ancestor, the gameplay is brought firmly into the 21st century.

The game is simple to pick-up and play. The basic tutorials are quick and easy and part of the actual story-arc. Hidden away in the missions menu there are also some extra advanced tutorials that you may want to check out.

For fans of Warhammer 40K the playing out the Second War of Armageddon is awesome. With each battle, more troop and armour options (and later Titans) open up. Each mission has a maximum number of turns and differing victory conditions to keep you entertained. In between some of the missions you partcipate in some dialog and your choices there influence parts of the game.

In terms of turn-based hex-based wargames it plays very similar to Steel Panthers World at War. You have predefined deployment hexes, and each unit has a visibilility range which determines if that unit detects an enemy unit. Units have various ranges for any ranged weapons they possess and have differing types of damage based on whether the target is armoured or not.

I have all the DLCs available at the time of writing but I have yet to play any so unfortunately cannot comment on them.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
Basic Overview: Alright, having played this game a few hours, it's fairly easy to sum this game up as the gameplay is fairly standard turn-based stuff. Basically this is very much like many of the classic turn-based military strategy games. You move pieces across a board, they kill each other, and there's a very complicated version of rock, paper, sissors based off of the Warhammer 40k universe. It's set on Armageddon during the 3rd Armageddon war.

Graphics: The close ups on the 3d models seem to be a bit unpolished compared to many games nowadays. This may bother some people but to me graphics isn't as imporatant as gameplay. Also, it's worth mentioning that units don't have moving animations, they slide across the board. They do, however, have firing animations.

Music: Honestly I can't remember any specific track from this game. The music is just kind of there, but it's not bad by any means, it just didn't stand out. Which may be what they were going for to keep it from getting too distracting.

Sound Design: As someone who works with music and audio stuff a lot. I really love the sounds in this game. They're smooth, tanks feel heavy, the firing sounds (to me) feel satisfying, etc.

Unit Selection: This game has a ton of different units to choose from here, infantry, walkers, vehicles, tanks, aircraft, artillery, titans (yes, you read that right it has titans in it! Quite a selection actually! :D), etc. Factions include Imperial Guard (including Armageddon, Armageddon Hive Militia, Ork Hunters and Penal troops, and Mordian Iron guard sorry boys, no Kriegs yet), Orks, and a few different Space Marine factions. As someone who fielded an Armageddon Steel Legion army myself, I'm very happy that they took the forefront in this game.  The variation in units may be overwhelming for some players and will undoubtedly confuse and perhaps frustrate someone new to Warhammer 40k. But let's face it, if you're throwing down $40 for a turn-based strategy you're probably hopelessly addicted to the 40k setting like most of us.

Gameplay: Sadly this is where the game sort of suffers. It's turn-based and does a good job of capturing the feel of the Third Armageddon War. But sadly while the game is a little entertaining, I mean I've put in a few hours and I plan on putting in a few more, it just doesn't have the umph that titles like Dawn of War have and I could easily see a lot of people calling this game 'boring'.

Story: While at first I was going to say I'm not going to spoil the story here, I realized the codex I first read this story from was printed in the year 2000, and that's sort of like saying I won't spoil the plot twist to Lord of the Rings... But that said, this pretty much is the story of the conflict of the 3rd Armageddon war and to hardcore W40K fans, it features some legendary names like Commissar Yarrick, Ghazghkull Thraka, and Herman von Strab.

Final Thoughts: After going through this game and playing it for a few hours. While I did enjoy my time, I don't believe this game is $40 good. The gameplay is sadly just too repetitive and unengaging to keep a lot of players captivated for a long period of time. I believe that for many gamers who like the more slow-paced turn based strategies, or want a classic style turn-based strategy this game will be a good fit. At least if they're familiar with the 40k Universe enough to know what the units are. If you're looking for an action-packed adrenaline rush, you won't find that here, and I'd strongly suggest other 40k titles like the hit Space Marine instead. All in all I liked the game, but asking $40 is a bit much to ask for what you're getting, but that's just how Game's Workshop is, isn't it? :3

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: It's Panzer Corps meets Wh40K.  If you love both, like me, it's a must have title.  That being said, the original asking price of $40 is absurd for what is basically a reskin of an already existing game.  I assume Games Workshop is at least partially to blame for the price.  It's a $20 game that you should try to get on sale for $10-15.  Space Marine DLC's have lots of content (units and a campaign), but Golgotha and Ork Hunters are a bit of a disappointment (just a couple short campaigns).

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
Warhammer 40k Armageddon is one of the few 40k themed games that stayed true to the tabletop game Warhammer 40k. The game is played in such a way that somewhat resembles and reminds me of an Epic scale 40k game or even a nice Apocalypse game. The texture and graphic are beautifully made with a plot that mostly resembles the story of the second war of Armageddon, where the heroes of humanity fought the brutal, mindless orks across the desert wasteland of Warzone Armageddon.
The game has very little to none glitches or bugs. It improvises a slow yet challenging gameplay. Every turn is a decision of life or death. Late into the game, you can even command legions of titans or baneblade heavy tanks and witness the epic clash of giants.
The game offers a huge variety of selection of troops including the Steel Legion of Astra Militarum, the Blood Angels, the Ultramarines and the Salamanders. You can fully customize your army to your likings, to the extent of altering the wargear of individual troops such as Gravity cannon, heavy bolter, incinterator or melta gun.
If you are a hardcore fan of Warhammer 40k, familiar with the rules of deployments and the strengths of troops, and seek for a game that transforms the tabletop apocalypse game into simple clickings, then this is the game for you.
The Emperor Protects!

Specs: Intel i7-6950X 3.50GHz CPU, Nvidia Quadro M6000 w/ 12GB GDDR5 VRAM, 128GB Corsair Dominator DDR4 RAM, Samsung 850 EVO 2.5" 2TB SSD. Running at 7680x4320 resolution.

The bad comes from a certain error that happens mid campaign for me, when you get to Hades Hive and meet with Yarrick. There is a error code that has not been addressed. I recommend this game but I also need to warn other players about it.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
Well.. I never thought this would happen, but this title sucked me right into hex based wargaming.

Honestly I'm extremely narrow minded with my strategy games. Most of the time you can lose my by saying "Turn Based"... let alone "hex based", but after watching a few let's plays this one lodged in my mind and wouldn't desist until I tried it myself.

It really does look like arse... like you're fiddling with a playset made of kitchen magnets, sliding them across an out of scale representation of this industry and war wracked planet... buuuut spend a little time with it and you gradually find yourself mentally filling in the blanks... noting the wee access ramps in the trenchworks; correctly ascertaining the changes in elevation. Yeah, it's a computer game that makes you use your imagination. 

And really there's no excuse for that... at full price it's forty bucks for a title without a single animation (other than some weapon effects)... but... it's good.

It's actually really good. Ineffably good in fact. Watch some videos. It will either grab you or it won't... if it does it might very well convert you to a turny, hexy gamer (if you weren't already).

annnnnnnd it's better than DOW3

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: Absolute CRAP. If you like Warhammer 40K, even to the slightest degree then DO NOT waste your money on this festering pustule of a game. it is NOTHING like 40K. The basic mechanics and gameplay is OK. Could use a bunch of small improvements like seeing a highlighted range/movement area before you move and shoot but it's not game breaking. What IS friggin game breaking is the B.S. unbelievable crap that infantry can do. Countless times I had a decent tank utterly destroyed by a pack of orcs with shotguns and cleavers. No, not heavy weapons or rocket launchers but goddam pea shooters and kitchen tools. This is just like the ludicrous stuff you see in the CIV games where some roman spearman can take out an Apache attack helicopter because...reasons. Also stuff appears out of goddam NOWHERE. You have extremely limited line of sightand many times your movement will be interupted as you come crashing into an enemy vehicle that you couldn't see. The game doesn't stop you 2 hexes short and ask if you want to continue, no, you just smack into it and auto attack if it's in your path, causing many expensive losses. It's great that you can earn experience but then again it doesn't seem to improve the units at all and they will all  die horribly before you get too good anyway. Even if you have a flying vehicle you can't see past 2-4 hexes. Sure these guys have interstellar travel but they have no concept of surveillance or even bloody binoculars. Oh and don;t expect Space marines or cool stuff like a Land Raider cos you're imperial guard in the Campaign so basically theres nothign cool. This is a ONE star game, end of story.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
I would recommend this to anyone who likes Wh40k and strategy games. For me it's the first time I've played a hex-based strategy game and I am very happy with my purchase.

- There's tons of scenarios. I'm six hours in and I haven't even finished Act 1. Even without the DLC you'll have plenty of content.
- The huge unit roster gives you a lot of options to customize your army. Also adds replay value. 
- Many difficulty settings, for newbs and veterans alike. I'm playing on Normal and it feels like a challenge. 
- Feels like a Wh40k game. Atmosphere is good and ofcourse you're playing in a very famous war. 

I rarely write reviews, but I felt like giving something back to the devs for this great game.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: From the little I played got the impression it's overly simplistic with very little strategy. Missions are so short and limit you to so few turns that it's just a matter of moving your units and hoping they can either rush past the enemy to achieve the goals or outshoot the orks/chaos forces due to sheer luck. It looks like that classic Warhammer Liberation game from the late 90's but that one had real depth to it, this is nothing like that. It's more like a mobile game where you move units and hope for the best in super-fast, extra-short missions. The story and voice acting are excellent, so it's a shame. 4/10

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
Fantastic Game! Can't speak for every DLC, but overall, this is a great game. I can only begin to theorize about why the critic reviews are lukewarm.

I think the game is beautiful, with amazing animations taking center stage for the visuals. They remind me of the explosions of world in conflict, that's how good they are. It is really fun to play the imperial campaign, and the story is certainly entertaining enough for a Warhammer 40k game, and the voice acting is quite a bit better than I would expect from an independent project like this one. The sound is a little less impressive, but it does a good job at setting the Warhammer 40k tone nonetheless. The tactics are good, and the progression through the campaign alongside your units is absolutely fun to say the least. Most of the time, missions are "more of the same," but that is a good thing since the tactics, combat, units and action are great. The game includes an amazing roster of units as well, which is sure to please any Warhammer 40k fan. I absolutely love how these units play and feel, and most of them are great fun to utilize.

That's not to say that there aren't any flaws. For one, the game is a bit unbalanced. There are certain units that are just so obviously better than others in terms of cost/benefit ratio that some of the units will, doubtlessly, just never be utilized, which is a real shame. Another thing I should mention is that mission objectives can be somewhat ambiguous on some missions, requiring a restart after an unexpected "defeat" screen. But this is rare, and thus not too bothersome. Another flaw often mentioned is the AI. I played the main campaign on "challenging" difficulty, and this is probably the one the game is balanced for. I think it's a lot of fun to play on this difficulty, but it is true that the AI sometimes makes quite questionable decisions which can be exploited to great effect. Then again, the enemies *are* orks, so what can one expect? It would be nice if I could change the difficulty after starting the campaign, but I have not seen the option. That is a problem too. Finally, there are some graphical bugs that can require a game restart, but really, I don't find that any of these things detract too much from the fun of gameplay during single player. Due to the imbalance, multiplayer probably wouldn't be quite the same amount of fun, however.

Overall, I think that this is a fantastic game. I will play the DLCs, and I will probably purchase the ork "expansion." I really hope that we will see more of the same content from the developers. I would love another game where we play Adeptus Sororitas vs Chaos or Dark Eldar, for example. Any combination of factions that aren't usually covered in these games would be amazing. I would gobble it up. But, for $40, the game is kind of a difficult sale, being such an indie title relying on such ancient game engines, so I understand why people might be so harsh on it. Still though, I had more fun playing this game than I have playing at least 60% of AAA titles. Honestly! This game is great, as long as you can look past the flaws and think of it less as a seriously competitive title, and more as a fun, challenging, and relaxing experience on a weekend when you have the time. If you are a warhammer 40k fan, and you can appreciate turn based titles, you owe it to yourself to get this game. It is amazing.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
Just let's keep this short.

You find yourself as the commander of an imperial defence army and have to kill orks to save the hive world Armaggedon. Fair enough.

You do so by commanding a small army chosen from over 70 different units consisiting of infantry, tanks, vehicles planes and artillery per faction. Each unit has its own combination of weaponry, so basically no two units are the same. The turn based combat is sometimes unpredictable and scouting is hard, since each unit has only one move per turn.

There are some fairly easy missions, and then there are some crushingly hard ones, even those that you are not supposed to win, long time no seeing that!

All in all I love it! One of the best turn based strategy games for me since the battle isle games on my old DOS computer. 

Completed the main story (Imperial Steel Legion-Campaign) in about 30 hours.
So far I did not play the DLCs.

Too bad it lacks a multiplayer and has some strange missing file bugs during cutscenes, which will give you a headache.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: This is an excellent wargame which balances interesting mechanics to model the the complexities of warfare in the 40k universe without becoming excessively complicated (like Gary Grigsby's War in the East/West). Each unit has a number of stats that determine their effectiveness in different situations: vision distance, initiative, armour, weapon damage, armour piercing, movement level/type etc. From, looking at the new 40k Gladius also from the publisher Slitherine I think I prefer this because the individual scenarios each with different objectives and maps seem to be more tactical with a deployment phase, with limited requisition points that carry over from mission to mission and are also required to reinforce your squads. Furthermore, the squads gain experience from their kills and loose it when some of the squad dies. Squads also carry over from mission to mission as long as they survive and within their unit category (Infantry, Vehicles, Walkers, Armour, Airpower, Titans) can be switched to different unit types as the mission requires. You therefore need to value the lives of your squads and learn to give them wise orders. All these mechanics, the huge unit variety along with varied routes within maps to complete objectives opens up a space for more strategic as well as tactical decision making. The Armageddon story is also interesting with good voice acting. I like the graphical style of the game which provides a pleasing form of abstraction like a board game. I wish they would add more campaigns, a skirmish mode different objective types against the AI and more races to the game. Like the Tyranid invasion of a Cadian Guard Jungle world. Adding Tau, Necrons, chaos Marines, Eldar etc would make the game even more awesome. Voice overs for the infantry would be good also 'For the Emperor!'.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
A good game for fans of hex-based tactical simulation or 40k.

The art style is quite detailed and looks good. Armageddon really looks like a grim, war-torn environment. Of course, the environments do get rather samey over the course of the campaign.

As expected in such games, you use terrain to your advantage and figure out which units work against which. Damage is predicted quite accurately so you won't face many surprises (unless you scout badly).

The story is presented in a mature way, with believable characters (orks will of course be orks). You really feel like you're in a desperate war against the orks, and the war feels like an actual war with proper strategy being used.

Mission variety is pretty good in Act 1, in Act 2 there's less variety.

Once you get access to space marine units and the high end Steel Legion units, the dynamic of the campaign changes quite a bit and you feel way more powerful. At least on normal difficulty, Act 2 felt far less challenging, but after being bullied by the orks for the entire first half of the game, I can't say I minded it too much.

AI will focus fire exposed units quite hard, but is also very passive in missions where they're the defender (on normal difficulty).

There is a big variety of units, which doesn't mean all of them are viable, but it's fun to play around with regardless.

The game has decent replayability if you want to attempt a higher difficulty, or make different choices (the choices aren't super impactful except for a few times where you get branching missions, but still worth exploring if you're curious about the different outcomes).

Every mission has a certain amount of 'requisition' and units unlock at set times. You usually have more than enough requisition to fill out all your army slots, which doesn't incentivise using cheap troops to get a bigger army (though you could do it to have more requisition left to replenish your wounded troops, I suppose) so the extent to which you manage your army comes down to trying to maintain veterans and choosing the 'right' units.

There's a good amount of content here, towards the end it did get slightly repetitive, once the 'tech tree' had been fully explored, and started to drag a little, but perhaps that's because I binged it and it did keep me engrossed enough to carry it through to the end with no major breaks between sessions (a very rare thing for me).

A solid 7.5./10. I recommend picking it up on a sale.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: I really like the game, but I'd like it better on mobile. It's fully 2d, doesn't take up a ton of space, and the turn-based-point-and-click nature of tactics games lend themselves really well to touch screens. The tactical depth is there, but the sprites are just "ok" and they don't have many animations. Now I don't care about graphics as much as game play, and the game play is great, but if I'm playing on PC, I have a ton of choices with both the tactical depth AND graphics. That is something much less of a concern on mobile devices, because even though they are capable of decent graphics, 3d tends to drain battery; so lower fidelity 2d is more desirable. I'd totally buy this game again on mobile.

review:
Its Panzer General (or rather Panzer Corps) but Warhammer 40K. If you know what that means and like both of those things its a no-brainer buy. 40K nerds will especially love the HUGE and I mean HUGE number of units. A dozen different types of Leman Russ tanks huge. Its cool.

A lot of the unit maintenance systems from Panzer Corps like fuel and ammo have been removed which honestly works to the game's benefit. I'm not here to plan a daring logistical blitzkrieg of Poland, I'm here to smash imperial guard conscripts against orks. Armageddon does that very well. 

DLC isn't essential. If you enjoy the base campaigns then being able to play as a few space marine chapters and even the orks themselves makes for a nice change of pace.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
An unfinished game that I would only recommend to die-hard 40k fans when on sale. Before buying, skim through this great guide by Cuddles to get an idea of how half-baked this game's systems are, and then decide if you still want it: 

https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=1089864462

Sure it could be enjoyable if you're looking for just any sort of strategy experience with a 40k skin... But if you're really bothered by [u]nonsensical design choices[/u] (there is no mechanic for "blasts", basic infantry can shred aircraft) [u]incomplete systems[/u] (none of the aircraft in the game are actual "flyers" even though the movement type exists in the game files. They "hover" and they can't hover over cliffs or mountains. The manual if full of references to other cut mechanics) and [u]not being able to see basic information[/u] in game (how much does this terrain impair my movement? why does my weapon do this much damage? what is my morale doing to my unit?) then i would give this game a pass. It has potential, and maybe a big fan patch could make it a much better game, but as far as I know there's no such magic bullet.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
This game is a hidden gem! I was not expecting much from this game and bought it cause I´m a fan of 40k and strategy games. When I first started the campaign I was taken aback for a moment, because the game has full voice acted lines for the briefing/story in the campaign, which I was not expecting especially from a slitherine published game (*cough panzer corps cough*). The game was really immersive because you can name the commander after you, and you are a new commander which has to prove himself on armaggedon hive prime. You have during the story some choices which alter the story (mostly battles). You can feel the desperate atmosphere of the last stand of the steel legion. You meet through the story Comissar Yarrick and other legendary 40k figures (I won´t say who cause I don´t want to spoil the game, but if you are familiar with the lore of the Wars for Armageddon you may already know who I am referencing). You will fight the Ork WAAAGH! and the Warlord Ghazghkull Thraka till your last men. You will have to sacrifice many of your soldiers. But in the end the EMPEROR PROTECTS!
I really wish they would make a second installment to this game with a fully voice acted campaign and choices you can make, be it trivial i don´t care.
BTW: there are 2 endings in the game, so be careful through your playthrough and watch out for heresy....

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
I was on the fence with this game for a long time. The main thing holding me back was the price. Holy pricey for a game with low production value batman! I read many reviews here and other places, browsed the Slitherine forums for ahwile, and decided to bite and fork over 50 hard earned dollars. Of course 4 days later it goes on sale 50% off! Needless to say, "steam" is coming out of my ears right now...but I digress.

The game is fantastic. Not quite as deep as "Panzer Corps", but deep enough to make you think about what you're doing. Do I pick up another support unit for my core force or another armor division? Do I deploy in one giant wedge formation or split my force into two battlegroups? Do I upgrade my artillery units to Deathstrike missiles for maximum effect against armor or do I keep my Earthshakers for better versatility? Do I use a turn to rest and refit my elite units or do I press on? Every mission had me asking myself these questions...the first time I played them of course.

The game has ZERO replay value; once you know how the scenarios will unfold almost all these questions will be answered for you. For this reason alone it's very hard for me to recommend the game at full price. If you're a big fan of 40k and wargames I can say this is a winner, but it's way, way, WAY overpriced. Wait for a sale, or get more value for your dollar and pick up another wargame.

I will close by saying that after 100+ hours of play I can say that most (but certainly not all) of the negative reviews I have read are from people who thought this was 40k tabletop on PC and are disappointed to find out that it's not, or are from people who are not wargamers and somehow ended up buying this. It's a solid wargame with no replay value and a way too hefty pricetag. If $50 is chump-change to you, then I recommend this to any wargamer.

One thing i forgot to mention...the "manual" is garbage. How can you not explain, or for that matter even mention "Zones of Control". This is an absolutely critical mechanic to understand if you want to succceed and enjoy a game like this on all but the easiest difficulty. If you're not well versed in wargames and are thinking of picking this up I strongly suggest you look up ZOC on forums or whatever. You will get alot more out of the game, trust me.

name: Three Sisters
review:
Before writing my review, one question burned in the back of my mind, gnawing at me even as I lay in bed: What does it mean to be a "three-shitter"?

I couldn’t shake it. The thought kept resurfacing, demanding an answer.

So I picked up a copy of the hit video game, Three Sisters.

The game hooked me instantly with its wonderfully wacky sense of humor. I actually pointed at my screen when the malevolent pale crab told me to do horrendous things to myself.

The game’s music—crafted entirely from some of the most horrifying noises imaginable (yes, those are literally real dying screams in the soundtrack)—somehow delivers a uniquely euphoric experience that no other game has ever matched.

The combat is flawless! Every strike from Vibia and crew lands with bone-crunching satisfaction - you can practically feel the impact rattling your screen. What truly elevates it is the progression system: with each battle, you grow noticeably stronger, creating that perfect "just one more fight" addiction loop. It's absolute gaming crack.

The story starts as a goofy romp about misfits committing crimes, then suddenly suplexes your heart into the pavement with moments of raw, unflinching humanity. By the end, I was laughing through tears at 3 AM, emotionally compromised but utterly in love with this dysfunctional family of losers. It’s the perfect blend of absurd humor and genuine heart—like if Tarantino wrote a D&D campaign.

This game shouldn’t work. A soundtrack made of nightmares, combat that feels like breaking bones with a baseball bat, and a story that swings wildly between dick jokes and existential dread? Yet somehow, it all coalesces into something transcendent—a messy, beautiful masterpiece that burrows into your brain and refuses to leave. It’s crude, profound, broken, and perfect. I’ve never played anything like it, and I’ll be chasing this high for years.

name: Three Sisters
review:
I have seen the face of God, and it lies Three Sisters deep within a world of raw, unfiltered slop.

A world that was built by unseen hands that know only obsession, each sprite is meticulously crafted with a grotesque artistry that defies reason. Every frame pulses with some incomprehensible force, like staring into the abyss and seeing it wink back at you. And the music? Calling it “music” is like calling a house fire “warm lighting.” It’s a powerful schizophrenia soundscape of warped frequencies, distorted hymns, and screams torn straight from dying throats. These aren’t just noises, they mean something, even if you can’t understand what. It drills past your ears and into the soft, unguarded parts of your brain, resonating with the soul in just the ♂ right ♂ way. It shouldn’t work. None of this should work. But it does.

The game does not introduce you to its world. It throws you into it headfirst and expects you to keep up. And you will. Because how could you not? You’ll scramble through its unfamiliar, deranged, dangerous areas, grasping at whatever sense of reality remains, clutching onto any scrap of logic you can find. You’ll meet its inhabitants, freaks, weirdos, liars, drug addicts, men who should be dead and yet keep talking. You’ll listen to them. You’ll laugh at them. And you’ll wonder if maybe they’re laughing at you.

Then, there’s the combat. It’s more like a manic bipolar simulator of desperate violence, a 3 AM gas station drunken brawl between your party and the horrors that stand in their path. You fight things that should not be alive. Things with too many eyes, or not enough faces. Laughing things. Hungry things. Things that are in pain and would very much like you to feel the same. Every hit carries weight, every well timed strike lands with a satisfying crunch that makes your brain light up like a malfunctioning slot machine. The progression is intoxicating, with each fight another desperate claw forward, another hit of pure, uncut power. The game knows exactly how to keep you coming back, even when you swear you need a break. There is no break. Only forward.

And the story? Oh, the story. At first, it plays out like a bizarre, somewhat schizo, over the top edgy yet goofy comedy, it's pure chaos. You laugh, because of course you do, it’s funny. But then, something changes. The absurdity isn’t just absurd, it’s real. The game reaches into your chest and grips something tender, something real. The laughter doesn’t stop, but it starts to feel different. By the time you realize what’s happening, it’s already too late. You care. You care about these characters, these losers, these nobodies who somehow feel more real than anything else you’ve encountered in a game. They are yours.

By the end, I was sitting in the dark, staring at the screen, unsure of what I was even feeling. The credits rolled, but I wasn’t ready to let go. The game doesn’t end. It festers. It lingers. It grows. Like a parasite. Like a blessing.

Three Sisters should not exist. It makes no sense. It is grotesque, absurd, emotionally devastating, and weirdly beautiful. It is wrong in all the right ways.

I have never truly felt joy before this moment. I’ve got to go now. I hear the Uber Crabs calling. Deep down, I always knew this would happen.

Praise Billy.

name: Three Sisters
review:
Three Sisters is genuinely unlike any game I've played before—it's strange, unsettling, yet oddly captivating. Right from the start, it immerses you into an eccentric world filled with bizarre visuals and characters who blur the line between humorous and unsettling. The artwork is distinctive, unsettlingly detailed, and strangely appealing despite its grotesque style.

Combat in Three Sisters feels chaotic and intense, pulling you into battles against uniquely disturbing foes. Every encounter feels impactful and rewarding, driving you to push forward despite the game's oddities.

The story initially seems random and absurd but quickly evolves into something deeply emotional and unexpectedly engaging. I found myself genuinely caring about the characters, each flawed and memorable in their own way.

In the end, Three Sisters left me reflective and intrigued, lingering in my thoughts well after finishing it. It's an unforgettable experience, balancing perfectly between disturbing and charming. Highly recommended for anyone seeking something truly unique.

[h1][b]Since nobody will read my review, here's a simple and basic chicken noodle soup recipe:[/b][/h1]

[h2]Ingredients:[/h2]
2 tablespoons olive oil or butter
1 small onion, chopped
2 carrots, sliced
2 celery stalks, sliced
3 cloves garlic, minced
8 cups chicken broth (or water with bouillon)
2 cups cooked shredded chicken (rotisserie or boiled)
2 cups egg noodles (or any pasta)
1 teaspoon salt (adjust to taste)
½ teaspoon black pepper
½ teaspoon dried thyme (optional)
½ teaspoon dried parsley (optional)

[h2]Instructions:[/h2]
[b]1. Sauté the Vegetables[/b]
• Heat oil or butter in a large pot over medium heat.
• Add onion, carrots, and celery.
• Cook for about 5 minutes until softened.
• Add garlic and cook for another minute.

[b]2. Add the Broth[/b]
• Pour in the chicken broth.
• Bring to a boil, then reduce the heat to a simmer.

[b]3. Cook the Noodles[/b]
• Add the noodles to the pot and cook according to package instructions (usually about 6-8 minutes) until tender.

[b]4. Add the Chicken[/b]
• Stir in the shredded chicken and season with salt, pepper, thyme, and parsley.
• Let it simmer for another 5 minutes.

[b]5. Serve[/b]
• Taste and adjust seasoning if needed.
• Serve hot and enjoy!

name: Three Sisters
review:
Greetings Traveler, 

you saw this game and though to yourself "lemme check those negative reviews and see whats up.." ?

Let me be your friendly guide!

Now i do have to make the disclaimer that i actually already wrote a review but was kindly asked to put it down since it got too detailed upon unpolished gameplay and other things- understandable, i take no offense to that... actually it is kinda cute? knowing that the Dev damn well is active and caring. I still want to give my unpopular opinion that nobody asked for but this time i try to make it feel less like a gametest/bug report.

Alrighty- 

The BIGGEST issue i have will be at the end so you do not get spoiled by my opinion while i try to be very objective through this review of the early access... and damn, there is content.

Here we FREAKI- is that crab telling me to hang myself?

Three SIsters is appearently a sequel to another Game, which i did not play because this is the first i got in contact with.. but what is it?

At its core (in my very wrong opionion) this is close to a classic JRPG- travel with your party through the world, collect key items and fight in a turnbased system (which is somewhat interactive) against your enemies! A
Also the soundtrack is great- but it is music and therefor you kinda have to like that kind of music? its a taste thingy... but i do like the soundtrack and appearently it will be released as DLC.

Here i have to make the comment that the combat is difficult, i will take a closer look at the individual characters and theire use later.

Exploring maps if you are patient and don't stress it is fun- assuming you like 2D pixelmap exploring alá FinalFantasy/GoldenSun.

The Story is there and i like it a lot so far. it isn't forced down your throat and it isn't consuming a lot of time which makes it a nice pace. The characters feel real enough that i can actually like them- no need to agree but like... or hate? approve. i approve of them.

The occasional Dungeon with the Bossfight and looting is there- and even if we are still early in development it gives enough to do some optimization.

now to my personal expirience with the game and i will not bother to hide spoilers- proceed on your own risk.

You start out with One char- Vibia- who can't do anything but swing her Spear

Your Attacks have to be timed with a ring which will be rated and effective accordingly- a neat twist to the rather flat Pixel-JRPG's you usually encounter.

Spells later on have a button mash mechanic for their potency- again, not reinventing the wheel but it feels different and makes you more invested!

Here i have to make a little disclaimer that the first Part of the game is ROUGH! not only because you probably don't know the game but also because of something called "Action economy" usually known among Pen & Paper players. but once you get over the hurdle you feel like you are playing the game... or against the game? its challanging you alll the time.

After beating the first hurdle you get your other two Partymembers:
Lucia- who seemse to be crying on her splashart even when smiling
Catherine- damn she is sassy.

Lucia is providing support spells and  she is very usefull for exploring maps, there are some nice items for her and she feels like the most sane person!

Cathrine is the chlichee white mage with heals, revives and let's you know that you are screwed without her, she has also the ability to annoy enemies. 
her res-spell heals the target to full hp! which is very good to keep you from reloading saves.
she does not have any noteworthy equipment and i have to admit there isn't anything worth giving her- not even the books that increase your Mind.

There is also "Extra" characters and some of them are weird...

now to stats which you can boost as you want by applying skill points- you get 15 per Lv and as of right now you make the decision what to focus fairly early.

this makes the early game after aquiring the three sisters (hehe, he said the title) easy but at somepoint (mexico) you just struggle.

I invested most of my points in HP except for Cathy because here revive is expensive.

Exploring is nice and all, and a little bit of puzzling is also aviable involving backtracking- hate it or love it, i love it.

The shop is neat, but there are items i can't figure out for the life of me.

The lack of savepoints is understandable if you want a hard game but be prepared to be suddenly steamrolled if you venture too far.

now to the biggest issue i have with this game and why it is so disgusting to me...

i never installed it.

this may sound like some ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ creepy pasta ♥♥♥♥ but it is the truth...

I never saw the storepage before, i never installed it... it just was there when i opened steam- already installed...

Is this a sign? are we living in a Simulation? Why is there a Stripper in the Crabcave?

Seriously tho... it creeped me out and that is the only reason why i don't recommend this game- anything else (assuming you like the genre and the crude humor) is top!

review: It's one of those older style rpgs with awful controls, except usually that gets made up for by something unique or interesting. This game feels like if a 14 year old discovered what the internet was like in the early 2000's and decided to base their entire personality around it. Really? A crab that tells you to kill yourself? A "drugs and hookers" cave? How revolutionary. Its like you're trying to implement edgy "dark" humor into a horror game atmosphere, except you're doing a poor job with both of those themes. Like another review said, I got this game for free like everyone else did and I still feel ripped off.

name: Three Sisters
review:
Gonna be honest, it's just not for me. The music sounded great so far. Reminded me of OFF a bit. The art looks neat (it's not AI generated for those wondering). Got past the tutorial. Fought a few enemies with only one attack with a skillbased "click when the circle is smallest" gimmick that didn't really change attack power or anything much. So basically just exchanged blows with intentionally annoying crabs that tell you to kys. One gave me a noose. haha nice. Get jumped by a gang of like 8 and are forced to fight them still having one attack. Nah, that sounds like a long slow and boring process. Use noose. Gameover. Realize there were no save points up until this point. Alt+F4. 

While I didn't have a good time with my time, I'm leaving a positive review because I know some will and for the price of free, try it.

name: Three Sisters
review:
Well I have various thing to highlight about this game, all positive btw.

Easy and familiar game mechanics if you have played other more popular games before. Character designs are very original, well made and funni. Like who tf would imagine an Ubers' car wheels would be the eyes of a crab.

On the other hand, this games comforts me. I like how its a mix of absurdity of mature themes with a drip of serious lore.As a person who is dealing with thought stuff I find this a really good game concept which even reflects irl human behaivour. Lots of ppl tend to laugh at their own traumatic situations as a way to relief to avoid constant overwhelming.

Is it worth the money? 
I must admit that if I never saw a random local online article that promotes low price/ free games I would have never discovered Three Sisters. 
I obtained this game for free and must say that is worth paying for it keeping in mind such things as:
* Is a game made with dedication from an small team.
* Frequent updates/ bug fixes.
* More gameplay will come someday.

Overall I find this game kind of unique and entretaining. Its just a game with joking manners with no "depth" most of the times. This review is kinda long cuz I like to get philosophical about anything and try to find meanings where they dont exist lol.

Just a quick reminder, its not the games' fault if you ignored all the warnings and started crying cuz you double-clicked Enter after an Ubercrab tells you to kys.

PD: It would be cool to have point decorations, I want little Vibias all over my profile.

name: Three Sisters
review:
Let me tell you a story. It was a crisp Sunday morning.
I had just arrived home, and I was sittin out on my porch smokin a cigar.
It was the last cigar I had, so I wanted to make it count.
I was doin a pretty fine job of that until, for just a moment...
I let go of the cigar while it wasn't in my mouth.
The cigar fell out of my hand and on to the porch,
Where it rolled off into the grass.
Upon realising that I didn't have any more cigars...
I was mighty upset.
I didn't have enough time to hike 15 miles to the general store.
The thought of not havin a cigar simply terrified me.
I immediately fell into a deep depression,
Distraught that I wouldn't get to have a cigar.
Less than four hours later, in a fit of cigar withdrawal madness,
I pulled a silly stunt.
Grabbed my trusty piece, played russian roulette all by myself.
Till the grim reaper showed up, and poof!
Just like that, I waved bye-bye.

name: Three Sisters
review:
Honestly, I'm not entirely sure where I stand on this game. A lot of it I enjoyed, and the story seems quite interesting, but the excessive cringe of the enemies (oddly placed against a less-than-crude story plot) also gets old pretty early on, and definitely pulls you away from it. 

The story also is a bit too light to give you a clear path forward. You'll pretty much just wander in circles, not knowing what to do, until you figure out where to head next. The variety of maps and settings is surprisingly nice though, so it's not a total misery to get lost. All the enemies respawn every time you leave the area, which is great for leveling up, and torturous if you're not looking to do so.  (Some police officers that respawn every single time you leave a house or shop, and which absolutely cannot be escaped, definitely come to mind.) 

Leveling up is also frustratingly vague. Its hard to know which characters actually benefit from certain areas being increased, as quite a few of the attacks/moves seem too ineffective to be worth it. I pretty much stuck to about 20% of the available moves once the characters were fully leveled, as the battles took way too long if I tried experimenting with other moves and strategies. Disappointing, as the game really does show a lot of different moves and has the potential to be a really fun experience there.

Also, I think the game developer might have some urine-based fetishes, given how often it comes up in game. Which is every other minute. I mean, its really a lot, even in terms of intentional cringe. It gets a little too gross and creepy there, that's for sure. 

Aaaanyways, I eventually I stepped away from the game, and I haven't been super inclined to try it again, not in it's current state. However, I did really enjoy playing it for a time, and especially enjoyed the sister characters and the world design, travel, and lore. At first, I didn't even mind at first that the enemies are so obviously, intentionally cringey. It just became a bit too much given how often you have to deal with them. Funny for a while, but it pulls away from the story too much in the end. 

This game definitely has a ton of potential, but I can't quite recommend it yet, not when it could actually be something legitimately good with a bit more time. Hoping the development with this continues, as there really is a lot going for it.

review:
I like the crappy visuals and the out-of-place music, but the banter is miserable.

In my first half-dozen combats, I had a crab tell me:

To Kill myself
That I was pathetic and it left the battle, granting me its XP (funny the first time, exhausting the next 12)
When it died it would haunt me, telling me to kill myself

Then the crab morphed into an Uber Crab and no matter how many times it duked me its XP and left, or I ran, I could never end the combat.

Easy uninstall, which is kind of a shame because I do think there's some neat stuff going on in here. I don't think I should have to put up with a Fornite lobby's worth of abuse to experience it - isn't there enough Fortnight lobby out there already? Perhaps the game creator could stand to tap someone else - hell, even ChatGPT - to come up with some better trash talk.

name: Three Sisters
review:
Dude like, this has to be the most niche game I've ever played, but the way it's so bizarre and sloppy kinda hooked me in??? I still wouldn't recommend this to anyone, but I can't say it's just 100% dogshlt. I guess I just happened to be part of the target audience.
Sometimes it IS interesting to look at a piece of media and just think to yourself "what the fuck is this?" and "what went through the mind of the devs?" like you have some edgy toxic crabs at one point, horrific realistic meat slop at another, then some horrific mexican clowns?? and they all look horrible, but for this game, it's its own charm. Then two seconds later the main characters will have some cool dialog or things happening that aren't shltpost-y and gives me whiplash.

I played for 17 hours, most of that was the very, very slow combat, which there is a lot of in the game; and many of which I had to fight multiple times because the enemy instantly killed me (which was funny the first times, but it became frustrating) and I had to reload a save, and go through the same 3-4 long fights before reaching some boss enemy and testing my chances again. Yeah if it was just the combat alone I prob would have dropped it earlier, but I was very interested in the world the game has, the different lockers kinda make me feel like i'm in Yume Nikki exploring the different worlds, I love that aspect of the game, sometimes I just wanted to grab cheat engine or overlevel myself just so I could "skip" the battles and explore freely.
Now I stopped playing because it felt like I've seen most of the game so far and don't see a goal or where to go or what to do, having my last things done being [spoiler] defeating Midas, and exploring some hills with bell guys (killed two eldritch horrors to get no reward at all haha) [/spoiler]

dude why did I play this for 17 hours...

name: Disc Jam
review:
Really fun game and quite addictive once you get the controls down. I wouldn't recommend playing this with keyboard as it can be quite tricking picking and choosing your shots using the keys.

The art style is funky as well as the music and the loot system drops every 3 matches or so. This game is nothing like Rocket League, the only comparison is that it's from a relatively unknown dev team.

This is a new game with only 2 devs bringing this to life, and it's already really good. I can see the potential for further content similar to how Psyonix (Rocket League) bring in new maps & games modes each season.

This game will blow up once it gets fully dedicated servers and an efficient competitive game mode.

8/10 would recommend.

Pros;

Fun & different
Responsive controls
Cool audio effects and music

Cons;

Dedicated servers should be the next port of call
Queue times could be quicker or matches should have more sets to last longer
The matchmaking can be a little ropey sometimes

name: Disc Jam
review:
** Edited to recommended **

This is a perfect example of a game that should've been Early Access. The price was too much for what you got, I think it's fine now.

The game is cool. You play 1v1 or 2v2 frisbee on steroids. Some might remember Windjammers from the past. It's essentially the same. You can dash around and do special throws with street fighter like inputs. Four different characters have their own values for speed, throw strength, etc and you can unlock costumes, tags, yadda yadda.

The only thing you can do is play online or practice throwing the disc at a wall. With a patch they added support for dedicated servers. Also, crossplay with PlayStation users is planned and hopefully arrives sooner than later. Meanwhile a system for skillbased matchmaking is in play and gets constantly tweaked and improved.

The game is fun. Bought it with a friend and we have a blast playing 2v2. There are issues though and this needs a bit more polish. But they are working on it. Many people compare it to Rocket League, feels more like SARPBC for now. I recommend it for the potential and the effort already done. There is a constant stream of updates and improverments.

tl;dr
+ windjammers
+ online/couch coop
+ fast skillbased game
+ short matches
- prize
- no real offline "vs bots" play

The prize machine is an okay concept although I have come to think that once you find a main character you enjoy playing with often and actually use very often, You dont seem to get very many new skins from the prize machine for the particular character. Could just be me here on this one. 

Although it is cool that every prize is only 1000 XYZ (Forget what the item is called...credits maybe) I think this games prize component would greatly benefit if a player was able to choose from a list of prizes rather than pull an arm and hope for the best. But this current mechanic is probably what is keeping most players coming back. 

________________________________________________________________________________

Final thoughts:

If you have 15 dollars burning a hole in your pocket and you cant find any game to satisfy the gamer in you......Go ahead and buy this game. Just dont plan on finding any opponents after midnight in your time zone.

review:
UPDATED!   After 90+ hours of play. Review still holds solid. Excellent Game. I still play every week, mainly in doubles with  friend.  I've updated the review a bit. Shorter, more precise.   


Disc Jam, a mix of badminton, frisbee and  PONG...that's right m***er f****ers.  PONG.

Devs say Tennis but nah I don't believe , in Tennis the ball can hit the ground...not in Disc Jam. Le Disc cannot touch the floor.


I will keep this review to the point.  

-  I played the game on a Free weekend, bought it for 8 $ cnd, it was half off.
-  I have now  90+ hours on this game at this time. 
-  I've played   215+   matches so far.   Game tracks stats  :)  
-  Only 1 map / court. It works fine. Developers have worked on better servers and such instead of a new court.  That is a good decision IMO, game works well.  New character added since launch :)


Pros

- Game is based on Skill. You play, you learn, you get better.    You  do  NOT  buy anything whatsoever that gives you an edge.

  It is purely skill based. You have no ability trees, no points to add, you just learn how to play and play.  Get better and learn.


- You  unlock asthetic stuff like costumes etc.  Cool stuff to unlock, tags, emblems, disc models (look only) 

- The developer is active on Steam.

- Very good server match making!  I wait from 0 - 3 minutes for a match.   US East / US West / Europe Queue.   

UPDATE:  Feb 8th a patch was released and Nintendo Switch version is out. 

- The game tracks a TON of STATS!  Matches played, Overall rank, Individual shot type ranking (curve, perfect, super, lobs), 
Win %  and a lot more!   You can split it from PC to Global view (cross platform). I'm currently  4th in Lobs on PC.  #selfbump

-  IT HAS CO-OP!    IT   HAS   CO-OP!         I've played approx 50+  Doubles matches,    INCREDIBLE FUN! ((many more doubles now after 90+ hours))

- Excellent stress relief 

====

Cons

- PC players are strongly recommended to use a controller. If you have an Xbox, plug the remote into the PC, if you have a PS3 / PS4 there is legal, safe software online that you can install to make those remotes work with PC games. Google is your friend.

** I played a good 20 matches with keyboard and while it was fun, with controller, it felt much more natural, optimised.**

I cannot think of any other Cons. 

==

Now my last point!

Read this review and watch the gameplay videos, I believe you'll enjoy it!

I recommend this game 10/10

name: Disc Jam
review:
Mechanically, the game is fantastic. The amount of different shots you can throw due to the slick controls is amazing. The concept is brilliant althought technically it has been done before (OG windjammers) I think that Disc Jam is the superior game of the two, the "from the back" viewpoint suits the game much better and allows for true 3d.
 The game suffers from 2 major problems.
 1: Lack of content. There is a training mode, offline vs bots/local player singles or doubles, and online singles or doubles. That's it. And every single one of them is pretty much exactly the same. No interesting new arenas or new mechanics or mutators.
 2: Lack of players. I think this is directly related to problem 1. Although the player count has improved slightly since I last played around 10 months ago I suspect this is mainly down the the game being free on PS+, seriously the peak players on steam last month was 17. And most days had a max of 10 at peak time. Also, PS4 players seem to be steamrolling the stats board. 
 This tells me that the game is really popular with most people who try it, but nobody is willing to blindly commit money to it. Which is a huge shame as, even with its shortcomings, this is a fantastic competitive Esports title. I don't know why the devs don't just make it f2p and work solely on new skins/customisations. Would certainly bring new PC players to the game and more than likely get a lot of currency purchases. Something has to happen soon though as the devs won't be able to afford dedicated servers for a game that's not making money somehow.
 Even with the utter lack of meaningful content, this game is a gem, and best played doubles with a friend IMHO. The lack of content is also reflected in the price somewhat, its only £11 at full price, I have 40 hours in it, and would have a hell of a lot more if I didn't stop playing at the height of playerbase troubles that's less than 60p per hour of entertainment. Anyone who enjoys 2v2 reaction + skill based esports really should give it a go.

name: Disc Jam
review:
This is a game that was reported to be easy to learn and hard to master.  It is not.  I have played Rocket League for over 1550 hours at the time of this review; a game that takes precise inputs at precise times.  In comparison, to say that this game's time windows for inputs are small is an understatement.  They're absolutely miniscule.  I literally had to look up an explanation on how to get through the "advanced" tutorial ("advanced" because no game mechanics in a tutorial are advanced, advanced mechanics build from tutorial mechanics).  

In tutorial it said to swing your analog stick REALLY fast in an entire semicircle AFTER hitting X to throw the disc.  You match the floor arrows AFTER hitting throw.  I had to look up an explanation that literally said "Because the time windows are so small many people find it helpful to start the motion BEFORE you throw the disc."  Which was the only way I could possibly get it to work, and even then I couldn't get it to do the full 180 degrees semi-circle.  

That's tutorial.  Let alone in-game.  It should be noted that this game ONLY FUNCTIONS IF YOU ARE ONLINE.  So yeah, there's that.  But once you decide to go in-game, you get matched against someone else who promptly ROCKETS the disc around you 9 times in a row until you lose the whole game while they dance and taunt on the other side.  Fantastic first game experience.  Learning curve?  More like learning cliff.  

I bought this to BLOW OFF STEAM.  NOT to invest 100s of hours to have a moderate amount of fun in.  This game is only for try-hards.  And all of this is being said without mentioning the loot box fiasco.  

Stay away.  This is a joke.

name: Disc Jam
review:
Disc Jam is a bit hard to review because the game is very bare bones, but what there is available is very good. The game is basically a mixture of tennis and air hockey where you try to score points by throwing a disc that bounces against walls into the opponent's court. The game, much like tennis, supports singles and doubles, but unlike tennis it also has a disc dodging mode that is more akin to dodgeball. The first two modes are the more interesting ones, but fun times can be had on all modes.

As for the game itself, I found it very addictive and relatively easy to pickup.  The game is mostly meant to be played online and, while many reviews state the game is dead online, I have not had the same experience. This may be because I allow for cross play with PS4 players, which are quite plentiful. In fact, I wait about 40 seconds or less to get into games. 

Also, there is a training mode and a mode called "Ghost Arcade" in which you play against bots that were made using previous player data. I find the AI in this mode is surprisingly challenging, to the  point that my score in that category is lower than my  best score in Season 1 (I ended up in gold)

In terms of potential grievances, characters and cosmetics need to be unlocked with in game currency that you earn by playing games. I would say that many may find the grind for in game currency to be unpalatable, and it will be needed to unlock 2 of the 6 available game characters. For reference, it took me a little over 10 hours t unlock one of the 2 characters, but I did spend about 10% of the characters cost on random cosmetics before realizing I needed the in game currency to unlock characters.

Another potential grievance is the perceived lack of courts, of which there are currently 2. My take on this is that tennis really has like 3 curt types, so it's not completely insane to have fewer court types.

Finally, one grievance I do personally have with this game is that the game doesn't have a "find next match" functionality,, s you can only either do a rematch or go to the main menu.

All in all, I think this game is well worth whatever I paid for it during the winter sale.

name: Disc Jam
review:
Great spiritual predecessor to Windjammers where you throw a disc and score goals in a manner similar to tennis. The game is really fun, but I have a few nitpicks: I doubt the microtransactions are bringing in much revenue at this point (and probably never were) and the online community is dead, so the developers should make it so everything is unlocked upon first launching the game. Also, when playing a private game with friends you don't return to the lobby post-game, meaning it's frustrating to play with friends since you cannot change teams/settings before each match.

I recommend it if you can buy it on sale and have some friends to play with. Content is slim and it's clear the developers are no longer doing basic upkeep, as there are still references to Mixer (a now dead streaming platform), and the most recent bulletin post is from March 2019; however, it's easy to pick up and games are quick, enjoyable, and intense.

name: Disc Jam
review:
The Good: A fun, solid spiritual successor to Windjammers that's easy to pick up and play and difficult to master

The bad: Built around a Overwatch/Rocket League model that requires multiple games to unlock anything, most of which are pallete swap skins, rarely used emotes, and easy to miss avatars/player titles. Or you can spend forever trying to get money to unlock the three extra characters. With a game built around online play, there aren't enough players to find a game regularly, except MAYBE on certain peak hours/days. You can play against ghosts of online players that somehow behave like the real players, so as long as you have some internet access, you can always play a game against bots, but without it, there's only offline couch co-op/Lan - I don't think there is offline bot play. These problems make it tough to recommend spending money on this game unless you get it on sale.

Oh, and it's one of those microtransactiony games, where you don't earn much out of standard play. The good news is that the highest cost currency bundle costs about $20, which I think will be enough to unlock the remaining characters and some loot boxes for skins and such, but i wouldn't be surprised if it didn't unlock everything. 

DiscJam is worth a purchase on sale, especially at less than $4. But the lack of players, near online-only requirement, and MTX model makes it tough to recommend @ full price.
