name: AFL Evolution
review:
I really wanted to like this game when it was first announced. But I did not make the mistake of setting my expectations too high. Anyone who has played any previous AFL game knows that we have never truly had a decent game to really sink our teeth into. 

So, do I like it? Yes.
It it what I was expecting? Actually better.
Are the graphics state of the art? Nope, room for improvement but it gets the job done.
So is this a game that you can sink your teeth into? I think so, yes. I think this game captures the AFL spirit and complements that with some decent gameplay. 

PROS
- Decent gameplay!
- Being able to evade other players when you have the ball.
- Career mode 
- Season mode
- Coach mode seems promising (I am yet to try this mode)
- Good detail in player creation
- Tribunal & awards
- Commentary - Does not have BT!
- Multiplayer and online play!
- Did I mention the gameplay is actually decent?

CONS
- Just like real life, the umpiring. 
- Certain game mechanics/physics (sometimes the ball goes through the post, or just misses it but is counted as a point)
- If you are clearly kicking to a player along the boundry and the opposition bumps that player away from the ball and the ball goes out of bounds, the Umpire calls 'deliberate'. Every.Single.Time! (Could also be a PRO)
- Graphics (For the eye candy "gamers")
- Commentary. Richo is as dull and boring as he is in real life. No personality and never really has anything positive to say! 

Ok, so overall this game is not perfect. But it is FUN. Hopefully the developers focus on keeping this game updated and listening to the fans. This game is a massive improvement from AFL Live 1 and AFL Live 2 (The worst in my opinion).

Look, if you are expecting FIFA style mechanics or graphics then you will be disappointed. This game gets the job done for now. It's worth a fair crack.

name: AFL Evolution
review:
So my first few hours of experience goes as follows:


"I started a couple of practice matches to get the hang of the game.

First practice match was AFL Women's team Melbourne Demons VS  the Under 18s team NT Thunder, in which the women won comfortably over the kids.

After only two matches I felt I was getting the hang of the controls, so I started a career mode. I created my character, a 28 year old 5'7" Right Half Forward playing with Gippsland Power in the Under 18s TAC cup.

A few games in, and everything was feeling too easy, the controls were getting pretty intuitive. Bump up to medium difficulty, and that seems to be decent, yet still easy enough to learn for now.

Eventually, we win the Cup, my character wins Goal Kick, Best & Fairest and the Morrish Medal. A 28 year old kicking ass against under 18s.

Being impressed by my character's ability to beat kids, Greater Western Sydney offer me a 1 year contract which I accept.

I play my first game in the big league. I notice that everything is much faster, running, kicking movement, everything has turned up to 11!

Regardless, I gather 20 disposals and kick 5 goals."


All in all, so far AFL Evolution is streets ahead of any previous AFL game, a lot of new features have been added and gameplay has improved.
Is this as polished a NBA 2k or FIFA? No. There is definely things that could be improved on, but I think considering the budget and experience those two franchises have compared to AFL Evolution, this is a decent crack at creating a decent game for our great National sport.

Do I recommend? For AFL fans, yes. This improved upon everything AFL Live had, and adds so much more.
For non-AFL fans, it's hard to recommend this for it's full price, when there's more polished games for cheaper.
I bought it because I love AFL, and want to support the developers and hope their next iteration is even better.

name: AFL Evolution
review:
I cannot recommend this game at this price. Which is a shame because I *really* wanted to give this game every chance. I heard the complaints of the console players (I had friends who traded the disc back in after three days - it was that bad), and waited two and a half months for the PC version to come out (with patches) so I could give it a go.

What I've experienced so far has confirmed the bad news. This just isn't a very good game, unfortunately.

NEGATIVES:

- Gameplay is clunky, rigid, and incredibly repetitive (especially in career mode).
- Like previous titles, the gameplay is just kick, mark, tackle, kick, mark, tackle... with the odd free kick here and there.
- The difficulty jump from easy to medium is insane. On easy with 5 minute quarters, I win by 10+ goals with ease. On medium, I'm lucky to even score anything.
- Graphics, while not important, are lacklustre here.
- Commentary is great for the first five minutes, after which you will hear the same thing over and over. Richardson's fake inspirational dribble at the beginning of every quarter is incredibly cringey.
- No drills/controls advice in free roam. You have to search through the controls menu yourself to learn how to play the game.

POSITIVES:

- The facescanned players look fantastic on the players hub. Though why did they bother scanning every player if they only used the results on the 5-6 star players of each team?
- The stadiums look pretty nice, I played a twilight game at Metricon Stadium and the sunset effect was glorious.
- The Bont is on the front cover.

I know this game was made on a low budget. I know that an AFL game can never ever reach the levels of a FIFA or NBA game. I know the sport itself is tricky to translate into a video game. But what is on offer here is just not good enough. If this is the best they can manage after so long, then I really doubt we'll ever see a good quality AFL video game.

Save your money. Pick it up if it ever goes on sale if you're absolutely desperate.

name: AFL Evolution
review:
Is console game, retro-fitted to play on PC. Little computer control capability, really desires a console type controller. Keyboard doesn't recognise all key-presses which makes even navigating the menues frustrating.

Game itself is buggy, computer takes control of player on some occasions and runs your player away from the action in contested ball scenarios. 

Testing the stats in Pro Career mode showed the stats aren't scaled and having the cpu play as your player with max 99 stats - he will still lose constantly, make stupid decisions and get out-played by all players in all positions. Playing as the player showed no notable differences between 50 stats and 99 stats.

I played a few hours of this game (though some of it were idle as I left it running during the day) and couldn't get a handle on the game at all. Controls are crap, game seems very clunky and not terribly responsive with the keyboard. Wouldn't recommend.

I understand that the game is the best AFL game to date (But that isn't really that much of an acheivement), For $50, it simply isn't worth it, it reeks of a bargain basement game of about $11.99 or so (atleast for PC - consoles may be different?)

Furthermore, the nicknames are bugged. All the nicknames I tested were something completely different in the game.

name: AFL Evolution
review:
I knew about the game months ago and was anticipating the pc release. I have played the Xbox One version for over 200 hours since May 5. Since release it seems to have many supporters and many that dislike it, and the tradition has continued over the past week with the release on Steam.  I appreciate the game for what it is, it allows me to play football. It requires a bit of patience to learn the nuances. People complain about the marking mechanics.......... I got the hang of it over time (yet I still can stuff up on occasion!!). I got the hang of the game pretty quickly, to the point where Legend RARELY poses a challenge for me (I have to "stop" trying, in order to let the AI score, so I have something to compete against) 10+ goal thrashings are common. 

I had a few teething problems installing the game and was almost going to refund it. Came back to it a few days later and it miraculously worked. Much to my delight as I was correct in my prediction that the pc version would be the ultimate version of the game. The difference with the frame rate from console to pc is mind blowing, I love how smoother it is during matches. 

Bottom line: This is the best AFL game since Aussie Rules Footy on the NES.

name: AFL Evolution
review:
Overall, if you love foooty, you will enjoy this game.  If you get it expecting EA Sports Madden graphics, gameplay, and commentary, you will be let down.  It's not on par at all with EA Sports.  

That being said, it is a comprehensive game, with the AFL, AFLW, VFL, and TAC Cup teams, along with all the great footy venues around Victoria and Australia. It is a fun game to play once you learn the controls and timing of the AI, but you will experience some blow out scores when you first start.  Keep practicing and you will get better!

All the rules are there, although not equitably applied to the player vs. the AI.  The AI will never get called for "holding the ball", and the player will never get "no prior opportunity".  You just have to dispose to a teammate because getting tackled is a turnover, which isn't necessarily how the game works in real life.  As mentioned by other reviews, the play-by-play commentary is abysmal.  That is something the developers need to improve drastically before they make the next version of this game.

I'm a massive footy fan, so I enjoy being able to play as my favorite AFL or VFL club.  If you're a footy fan, you will enjoy this more than you won't.

 Even there. some things need tweaking by the Devs. Clubs patience with a new coach should be softened, no real life bottom ladder club signs a new coach and expects them to play in a Grand Final or even finals in their first year. Also, AI clubs should, I feel, be much more agressive and less accomodating come player draft/trade time.

TBH my biggest criticism of the game is that Carlton seems to make finals every single season. Seriously????

Don't get me wrong, Evo has a lot of bugs and it's gotten to the point that my saves won't save anymore but at its peak i had a lot of fun with this game. If you are a forgiving person and can ignore a lot of the bugs you might also enjoy this game, especially with mates it's a pretty dumb game you can get some form of joy out of it. Plenty of sliders to adjust to change the entire way the game plays too. If you're looking for a realistic AFL footy sim, this game won't be the one for you at all chief.

There are probably more negatives than positives to this bad boy, but it's a decent footy game that I still load up on occasion to play a few games because sometimes you just want to see the St Kilda win a game for once y'know?

name: AFL Evolution
review:
I absolutely recommend this game, but with a caveat: you probably need to be a huge fan of Australian football. This is not an EA game. Regardless of how you view EA, their game-play does have quite a bit of polish on it. AFL Evolution feels like how sports games felt when they first made the move to 3D. The controls require a bit of effort to get used to, the player movement feels a little wonky, and the animations are off. It isn't rare to watch players not only clip into each other, but to full on walk though ghost-like during non-play animations.

But, when it comes to the more exotic codes of football, Australian football is only above Gaelic football for game representation. I buy these games as a way to tell the developers "good effort, keep trying". I feel there could be a good quality footy game somewhere just beyond the horizon. If you're a fan of Australian football, especially an international fan that couldn't possibly satisfy their hunger for more, this game is a fun - albeit messy - game.

name: AFL Evolution
review:
I have played both AFL Evolution 1 and AFL Evolution 2 and I think that even though 2 has updated graphics, different stadiums and updated line-ups/fixtures, I think this game has more content. Sure, graphics may not be as good number 2, but the detail in manager content is really nice.

- Firstly, in Pro Player, the points system is way easier to understand. The higher your skill level is, the more stuff costs. Sure its the same in 2, but that goes into the thousands, whilst 1's prices go from 1-10. There are more options to spend training points on. When you sign a contract for an AFL team, you get a low to high salary price, depending where that team placed that year and what year you'll be playing until. I like how it give those small details.

-In Coach Career, you get to choose who you staff, who your sponsor is, and you can chose your team playing style and lineup. The only downside is that if your team places below 12th, you instantly get sacked and have to coach a VFL team. The draft is good, because you get the draftee's current skill level and their potential skill level. The training is really good, being able to choose different training types for different positions. The offseason stuff is good, being able to make contracts for players for up to 5 years, although there is an auto-contract option for those who are too tired to go through every player. Drafting is alright at best, teams can sometimes be really picky when you give them an offer. (E.G: I offered the Bulldogs Pick 4, Jarryn Geary and Paddy McCartin for Marcus Bontempelli. They said it was "Almost Satisfactory"). But if you're willing to let go of a pick 1-3 draft pick, and a rated 87-90 player for a player rated 90-95, its well worth it to make the superteam you always wanted.

Even though this game is 5-6 years old as of writing this, I think that if you don't want to spend the $70AUD for AFL Evolution 2, $50AUD is really worth it for this game.

name: Trials of the Blood Dragon
review:
I want to say out of the gate that a I think it is very unjustified and unfair to give this game negative reviews for not being a Trials Fusion clone, additionally, these complaints are coming from people with less that 30 minutes in game. Trials of the Blood Dragon like Far Cry 3: Blood Dragon was never intended to be a successor or continuation of the parent game. If you were expecting that you should look elsewhere because you will likely be disappointed. Aside from similar gameplay mechanics Trials of the blood Dragon like Far Cry 3: Blood Dragon was supposed to be a fun departure from the main game and I believe that is exactly what they have done.

I went into this game expecting what I got from Far Cry 3: Blood Dragon, which was an absolutely ridiculous, over the top, stylized, comedic parody of 80s video games and action films, and that is exactly what I got. The game features anther excellent soundtrack from Power Glove which really compliments the exaggerated 80s style of the game. In addition to the new look Trials of the Blood Dragon adds new gameplay mechanics such as shooting from your bike and on foot segments. For some people these new additions may be a hit or a miss since it is a departure from what fans of previous trials games have come to expect. The bottom line is that if you go into this game expecting a Trials reskin you are going to be disappointed, for the price you get a lot of content and style for what you are paying for. Fans of Far Cry 3: Blood Dragon and its over the top 80s look should feel right at home with Trials of the Blood Dragon.

Pros:
Fantastic style
Interesting additions to gameplay
Great amount of content for the price
Another amazing soundtrack from Power Glove

Cons: 
Some new additions such as on foot segments can be tedious
(Subjective) A significant departure from past trials gameplay

name: Trials of the Blood Dragon
review:
Trials of the Blood Dragon is a nice departure from the Trials series. If you are lookig for a normal Trials game, then this title is not for you. However, if you are open to changes, then I highly recommend this game. There are a few tedious sections here and there, but you will be able to get over it and enjoy the game for what it is.

Like the normal series, the Trials sections get more and more difficult throughout the progression of the game. Sadly, I felt as though it was not super challenging until the very last mission. So I blew through all of the Trials missions with ease, but I still had fun because it's just like Trials! Every other mission was either 'okay' or 'good', nothing bad to come from this game, really.

I have never played Far Cry 3: Blood Dragon (FPS isn't really my cup of tea unless I'm playing multiplayer with friends), but I thought that I should probably give that a look after playing this game. The aesthetics are super nice and the music is VERY good.

If I had one complaint, it's that this game is way too short. I beat this in only two and a half hours and there's no real replay ability in it. It would just be the same missions over and over again. I thought that this game would be a lot better with a level editor or something to go with it so that people can create and share their own missions. That would make me want to play this game more and more.

In conclusion, this game is really fun. It has a fantastic soundtrack and a pretty vaporwave art style. I do not recommend getting this game for $15. If this game goes for sale for around $5 then you should definitely pick it up. It's short and full of adventure!

name: Trials of the Blood Dragon
review:
Why did Red Lynx waste so much time on this when they could have been working on a sequel to Fusion? This is not a Trials game foremost, but most of all, it's not a good game period. It bases its spirit on Trials but loses the entire point and becomes a jack of all trades for a hodgepodge of different game types haphazardly assembled together. Do you like absolutely awful, floaty side scrolling shooters with the worst enemy A.I. you've ever seen? Do you like pointless distracting additions to trials tracks like shooting targets instead of focusing on time and technique? Do you like weird RC car segments with frustrating controls that reverse on you? Do you like excessively intrusive story sequences in the style of esurance commercials and cheesy throwbacks to absurd 70's action movies? Well Blood Dragon wants to do all sorts of things, none of them any good and all cobbled together so you can't separate the ones you don't want to play. 

It's just not a good game and we could have been that much closer to a proper Trials sequel if they didn't get on this weird DLC bandwagon for introducing ridiculous internet humor inspired remixes like riding a unicorn. They lost focus on the core of Trials, hopefully they turn it around.

name: Trials of the Blood Dragon

I enjoyed the voice acting, and I think the game is very pretty, for what kind of game it is. However if you aren't usually a fan of the sidescrolling biker games, prepare to be frustrated. And ultimately.. While I really enjoyed the story.. For this price, I wouldn't really recomend this game to others unless you REALLY like this genre.

name: Trials of the Blood Dragon
review:
TL;DR: Buy if you're a big fan of Trials, wait for a sale if you're not. 7.5/10. It's really fun and has some decent replay value. Eff the haters who only have 20-30 minutes on record giving negative reviews.


If you honestly believe the negative reviews of this game then you should think about who you trust. People down here dissing this game for saying it's so short and easy and they only have 20-30 minutes on record. This just shows you that they haven't even started to play this game. I am an expert trials player and i just finished the game at the 3.7 hour mark. I got 100% gold or platinum on every track in trials fusion in under 2 hours. This is a really cool game and refreshing to say the least. It is certainly not a standard trials game. I was hoping for some time trial maps and expert difficulty tracks. Since this game doesn't have that it doesn't take away from it. This game wasn't meant for that. Instead of me instantly coming to write a negative review, I looked down and noticed that i wasn't a douche bag so I decided to review the game for what it is. It is a VERY fun 3-4 hour game with moderate replay value down the road. If you don't like trials you should wait for a sale but if you like trials or fun, silly games pick this badboy up. In all seriousness as a trials fan I'd give this a solid 7.5/10. I'm not IGN so 7.5 doesn't mean its the worst game of all time. It's really cool. Pick it up!

name: Trials of the Blood Dragon
review:
Okay here's the deal.

There are much better trials games and MUCH better platformer games.

If you haven't already extensively played and enjoyed previous trials games, leave and come back to this review when you have.

Okay now assuming you are a trials veteran, here is what to expect. This is coming from someone who has 150+ hours played.

The story mode will be completed in about 2-3 hours.
This consists of a collection of 26 levels, which are a mashup of some beginner tracks, a couple jetpack skill games, a couple minecart skill games, some trials tracks with a grappling hook, and some platforming levels with truly terrible controls/physics.

The game has a goofy stupid story, visually the levels are good, and the music is great.

Throw in some bonus "extreme" levels that are a bit more difficult.

There is no Track Central, this is essentially just a standalone dlc for trials fusion.

If you think the game doesn't offer enough challenge, just try and beat me on the leaderboards lol.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Why do I have so many hours played you may ask?
Well a lot of the fun in the game for me was that this game came out during a content drought for the trials community, and I had a lot of fun going back and forth with different people in the community competing on the level leaderboards, finding new strategies, etc.

I would not expect anyone else buying the game at this point in time to get much more than maybe 6-8 hours of playtime out of it.

name: Trials of the Blood Dragon
review:
PROS:
- A lot of variety in gameplay
- Interesting level designs and changes
- Story has its funny moments
- Great Set Pieces

CONS:
- Short as all hell
- Pretty easy compared to other Trails games
- Voice acting is BAAAAAAD

I was kind of surprised to see this pop up on steam, I honestly thought it was one of those shitty rip-offs that slide in through greenlight. Turns out this is all legit and once you actually start playing the game that becomes pretty obvious. The production values for the levels are way ahead of any of the previous Trials games. With most of them featuring at least one flashy set piece.

Of course the downside of that is that you end up paying more attention to the background and crash a hell of alot. In fact one of the biggest problems with the game is the fact that it mixes so many playstyles in together. In a single level you can go from driving a Tank to a motor bike to a Jetpack. Which is cool but does mean that you are not likely to get anything approaching a good time the first time through a lot of levels as you wrestle with the widely different handling of each mode. 

One of the other downsides to the more polished and flashy levels is the length of the game. Even with a bunch of deaths I still beat the game in about two hours. Obviously I will go back and try to ace the levels which should double the time but compared to the length of Trials Fusion this is depressingly short. 

Along with that the game never reaches the challenge of Fusion. You never really have to be that precise with your controls and it's only in the last level that you start to face more advanced challenges.

Having said all of that there is still a bunch of stuff I have yet to do in the game outside of the story missions. There are five secret keys to find each with a clue to where it is along with rewards for acing levels. 

For £11.99GBP I think it is a tight little package. If you go in expecting something on the scale of Fusion then you are going to be disappointed but by the same token there are no levels in dull static warehouses to grind through. I like Fusion but it was pretty padded in some areas and this game doesn’t do that. Choosing to cut out all of the fat to give you pure spectacle.

name: Trials of the Blood Dragon
review:
Trials of the Blood Dragon has a lot in common with its infamous E3 presentation; a great, bold, different idea on paper that turned out terrible in execution.

I came into this game expecting very polished level design as seen in the previous Trials games. Trials HD and Trials Evolution both had a great difficulty curve, tons of customization, and different bikes for you to choose from. This game offers none of that. You are set in a 27 mission single player experience. No different bikes. No customization. No user created content. No multiplayer. For the same price point as Trials Evolution, this game is an absolute joke when it comes to content. I was able to complete the *entirety* of the game in under 2 hours.

Plot: 

The idea of an actual intertwined story that links the missions together, rather than levels being discrete and separated from one another is a great idea. I don't know if I really needed a story within a Trials campaign, but the idea is great. However the execution on this story was awful. The plot is neither funny nor interesting like that of Far Cry: Blood Dragon. The two main characters have very stilted dialogue and poor voice acting.

Mechanics: 

There are three new mechanics added to this game over previous entries in the Trials series. Enemies and weapons, a grappling hook, and... on foot platforming.

The ability to use an smg on your bike is by far the best mechanic added. In order to complete races as quickly as possible, the smg allows you clear a path by shooting enemies, or by shooting switches in order to open doors. This provides a more flexible level design, and seems like a very smooth addition. The grappling hook functions in the same way as the gun, but you use it to reach certain vertical areas. I feel like this addition to the game was not as well executed as the machine gun's, as it removes any and all challenging platforming sections that were available in previous games.

Finally... on foot sections. God... What do I even say. This was an absolute mistake to add to the game. Clunky, slow, repetitive platforming that is not satisfying and NOT what I would want out of either a Trials game or a Far Cry game. One weapon. One enemy type. Not enjoyable in the slightest.

Final Verdict:

In theory, a cross between Far Cry Blood Dragon and the Trials series sounds like a wonderous time. Gun toting, action packed biking. Instead, we get an incredibly short, repetitive, unchallenging, and not too fun game that's mediocre at best.

name: Trials of the Blood Dragon
review:
80s themes? Yep. Hotline Miami references and homage? Yep. Kickass soundtrack? Hell yeah. Trials? Oddly, yes.
Certainly not a crossover anyone saw coming, and definitely not everyone's cup of tea. Despite this, I enjoyed the game greatly. Lots of setpiece level design, each one is very unique, I even saw some building techniques that weren't in other Trials games. Honestly, this isn't just a Trials game or a sequel to Blood Dragon, it's a blend of both. I recommend this if it's on sale, because I certainly enjoyed the story and cheesy writing.

name: Trials of the Blood Dragon
review:
[h1] The Rundown [/h1]
After the events of Far Cry 3: Blood Dragon (amazing game, check it out), Rex Power Colt has had two kids: Slayter and Roxanne, who have been tasked with dismantling the Empire of Vietnam, ending Vietnam War IV. You commandeer different vehicles depending on the course, and sometimes even complete missions on foot as either character. Just like Blood Dragon, it tries to pull off an OTT 80s style.

[h1] The pros [/h1]

[list]
 [*] The soundtrack in this game is great (but this could be my synthwave bias talking)
 [*] Tries to keep the Trials feel of difficulty in the motorbike levels.
[/list]

[h1] The cons [/h1]

[list]
 [*] Some of the jetpack levels are silver surfer tier difficult.
 [*] On foot is kind of clunky. While I didn't have much issue, I can see where others are coming from.
[/list]

[h1] The verdict [/h1]
In my opinion, the game needs LOTS of work. The aesthetic and soundtrack are fine, but controls for jetpacks are unbearable, and for the standard player, probably a put-off.
As a result, I [b] cannot recommend [/b] this game, sorry.

name: Trials of the Blood Dragon
review:
First off, I've played Far Cry 3: Blood Dragon and all of the Trials games.

The story is just not believable, not even to a 10 year old kid. There are a few jokes here and there but none of them are very funny.

If you're expecting a game like Trials Evolution or Trials Fusion, you'd most likely be disappointed. The game-play is just not what you would expect from a Trials game. Half of the time you'll be playing as one of the kids in a 2D 'run and shoot' game and other half, you'll be playing in a 2.5D mode in Trials game-play with a few twists, I'm trying not to spoil too much. If you're a fan of the previous Trials games, then chances are that you won't be liking it at all.

The blood dragon story was totally unnecessary. There were several blood dragons in FC3:BD but in this game, there's just one? It's more like trying to kill a Godzilla. The story would be much believable if the developers had replaced the whole blood dragon concept with another monster and had left FC3:BD out of this game completely. After you unlock some of the hardest levels, you'll probably need a controller if you hope to complete them.

Whether you're a Trials fan or a FC3:BD fan or both, I wouldn't recommend this game to you. This game is a complete disaster.

name: Trials of the Blood Dragon

As a big Trials fan, this game was a fun diversion after how lackluster and reduntant Trials Fusion was.  It's really short and lacks content (I beat it in less than 2 hours), but for its sale price it is worth it and it is fun.

The hate the non-Bike sections get are overblown.  There's only one level that is really bad.  The rest are fine and fun.

The writing and story are pretty terrible, but that's what the skip button is for!

If you want a more casual Trials experience, this game is legit.  Not amazing, but a good time waster if you can get it on sale.

name: Trials of the Blood Dragon
review:
Haven't played rising yet, but compared to all of the other Trials games this game is the worst one for many reasons. The main issues I had when playing through the game were the gimmicks, the difficulty, and the "story." I'll go into more detail about these issues below.

1. Gimmicks
For some reason they thought it would be fun to add gimmicks like minecart levels, guns, platforming, and grappling hooks. Some are alright, but most of them are complete garbage. For instance, the minecart stages tend to have this issue where even if you land on the ground perfectly, your cart may sometimes flip over anyways and lead to you losing. The biggest gripe I have is with the grappling hook. When you go to the Japan themed area of the game, it heavily relies on grappling hooks and it shows how annoying this gimmick is. Another good are in the game that is great at showing how flawed the gimmicks are is the Mars/Hell themed area since every level in the area has some gimmick which is the focus of the level.

2. Difficulty
Trials games are known for being difficult, but this game falls flat. Yes, it can be difficult at times, but usually it is due to a previously mentioned gimmick being used in the stage. Other than that, most stages are Easy to Medium in difficulty with maybe a piece of a track being a bit harder than the rest. There are 3 stages which show off the bad of this game pretty good. First is a stage where the gimmick is that you have to carry a bomb on a trailer attacked behind you without making it explode. That might seem annoying enough, but the second half of the stage has you carrying the ball while using a jetpack which is just a complete mess. The second stage is the final Japan themed stage where you have to "fight" the 4 ninja characters and my god it takes so much time for a single stage, so if you are going for an A or A+ rating good luck with that. Finally is the final stage, which is the most lackluster stage in the game. Basically, you drive up and down mountains with ease for 8 minutes with the occasional jump, and then for 2 more minutes you do some of the platform/shooting section which can have some cheap moments where you get shot by an enemy you couldn't see.

3. "Story"
I get it is a parody of the 80s or 90s or some crap, but most of it just seems to be "lol random funny xD" which is fine I suppose. The big issue though has to be the ending. The ending (and secret) ending rivals that of the campaign ending of CoD Ghosts in that when you complete it the game practically spits you in the face and gives you an unsatisfying ending. I then looked up the "secret" ending, which literally adds a few more seconds which just says fuck you to the players a bit more.

So yeah, this game is hot garbage full of poorly made gimmicks which I hope never reappear in the Trials games. Maybe get it when on sale for dirt cheap if you really love trials and want it in your collection, but even then I highly recommend just ignoring this game.

name: Trials of the Blood Dragon
review:
So glad I got this game. I really liked Far Cry Blood Dragon and this is a fantastic addition to the mini-franchise spinoff. The intro is hilarious and the game is full of parody and satire, perhaps more so than the Far Cry game. Just like Far Cry, it is full of epic action and you really feel like a badass when you finish a level with no faults and a good time. The perfect run is elusive yet the gameplay and controls are so fundamentally simple that it's addicting. Furthermore, there's no waiting to restart, it's instant. The art style and music are excellent, as you'd expect, but so are the visuals. It's surprisingly more demanding than I expected at 4K max settings, I get some dips below 60  FPS with a GTX 1060 6GB but overall it's moor than playable and looks decent - better than Hotline Miami at least! Speaking of which, one of the chapters seems to be a direct competitor / parody what with the on foot sections and enemies, level design, masks etc.
Sure, there are some gimmicky and annoying sections where you're not on a bike but when they do get it right, and you have a near-perfect run, the game shines. Leaderboards also allow for near-endless replayability.
I honestly don't understand how this game got rated so poorly.  Maybe it's just my kind of game, and this is coming from someone who doesn't follow the Trials series. If you want something satisfying, simple and that is pure skill (i.e. no RNG), even if the skill cap is fairly low, it's a fun game to hop on. The levels are just the right length in terms of the balance between difficulty and content: they're short enough to make you want to replay them and not get frustrated when you fail, but long enough to make it very difficult to get a "perfect" run.

name: Trials of the Blood Dragon
review:
I'm not too familiar with the Trials and Far Cry games, so to me, this an all-new experience.  Take my opinion for what it's worth.

GAMEPLAY:  
--- Basically you control a pair of characters through obstacle courses either on bike, foot, tank, and other various means.  
--- All movement is in 2D, but the backgrounds and stages are 3D rendered.  
--- The goal is to navigate around the obstacles and get to the finish line in the shortest amount of time possible, after which you get graded on your performance. 
--- Memorization of the traps and stage layout are key to getting a good grade.
--- Controls are very simple, involving a button for acceleration, braking, and left/right directionals for tilting your vehicle (think Excitebike for the NES).  Similar controls for foot missions, but involve jumping/shooting/platform elements.  Simple to play, but tough to master.    
--- Lots of unlockables and hidden goals in certain stages.  
--- High replay here if your aim is to be the best and unlock everything the game has to offer.  Otherwise, expect 5 or so hours to finish your first time through.
---  Supports either keyboard or controller inputs.

GRAPHICS:  
--- Looks sharp and runs nicely on my system, which is 5 years old.  As usual, visit any game's Steam page to ensure your system can handle it.
--- Vibrant colors reminiscent of the 1980's to help capture that decade's feel.
--- As mentioned before, movement is 2D, though the camera angles may change somewhat depending on the stage.  
--- Intermission cutscenes between levels use a kind of cartoon-like animation with 80's inspired elements.

SOUND:
--- Lots of 80's inspired synthwave tunes to help set the tone.
--- Explosions and sound effects sound great on my surround sound setup.
--- The dialogue (and story) can seem a bit childish at times, but might erupt a chuckle or two depending on your sense of humor.

OTHER NOTES:
--- A UPlay account must be created and active while playing this game.
--- Certain DLC items can be unlocked by earning UPlay coins, which are acquired after performing certain achievements

FINAL VERDICT
--- I found this to be a lot fun for what it was.  I picked it up for less than $5, and couldn't be happier with my purchase.  
--- It's one of those game I'll definitely keep coming back to in order to improve my grades and get better times.  
--- If you know nothing about Far Cry or Trials, it's still a simple enough game to pick up and learn without feeling lost.
--- Recommended for those folks who love dirt bike racing games, platformers, explosions, and music/animation with a 1980's feel.

name: Trials of the Blood Dragon
review:
This game is the perfect example of experimentation over refinement. This review comes from a huge fan of Far Cry 3: Blood Dragon, and someone who has dabbled in and played a few of the Trails games in the past. Seeing as I loved FC3BD, and had played some of the Trials franchise, I figured I'd give this one a try. This game was something I had wanted to play for a long time now, but the mixed reviews scared me off from it at first. Luckily, I caught it on sale and...well I'm glad I did.

This game is filled to the brim with experimentation, and has several styles of game-play. It has biking sections (sometimes either with a gun or a grappling hook), truck driving sections (which there are very few of), jet-packing, boss fights, and even on foot gameplay. And what's absolutely absurd is that there are several different subcategories of these various play styles, which change up gameplay even more! My take on the gameplay in a short break-down:

Biking - Hit or miss, but it's mostly fine. The physics seem to have gotten worse from the previous Trials games, which screwed me over a few times, but it's not the worst controls I've ever had to play with. The grapling hook can be pretty jank at times, but I must admit that the gun gameplay merging with the speed of the bike was some pretty great stuff. The level design isn't bad for the most part though, and when the level design is at it's best, it can be fun to zip through these levels, especially with the game's great aesthetic.

Trucking - Probably my favorite, but the only reason being that I don't have to deal with the sloppy or finicky controls that the other playstyles offer.

Jet-packing - Have fun trying to get past lazer beams while flying in this thing.

Boss fights - There was one boss which I actually think was decently enjoyable to fight, but other than that, the bosses felt incredibly frustrating.

On foot - In a Trials game? Why? Just...why. The platforming is not only basic, but very unsatisfying. I don't quite know how to describe it, but it doesn't feel like your character is jumping; more so it feels like your character just levitates in the air as you hold the button, and it's one of things where you really have to experience it for yourself to see what I mean. Anytime I would get into one of these sections, I would roll my eyes and hope to get back to the next biking section.

Now onto the story (spoiler free). It's not that great to be honest, and everything feels so disjointed and hard to follow. Granted, FC3BD didn't exactly have A tier writing or anything, but the game had a very good sense of humor to it, and was at the very least able to keep cohesion all the way through. There were very few points throughout Trials of the Blood Dragon that actually made me laugh. There are multiple twists to this story, which was a pleasant surprise, but these twists have little to no foreshadowing whatsoever, even in spite of them making sense in context of events.

The soundtrack is just fantastic. It is done by Powerglove, the same people that did most if not all of the tracks from Far Cry 3 Blood Dragon. Their work was phenomenal in the previous game, and their work still shines bright neon here as well, but with this game they're really able to show off a lot more of their potential. With the constant fast and action-packed pace that this game tries to portray, they were able to do a lot more with what they had. What's interesting is that I could swear that my ear would pick up on some remixes from FC3BD here and there, which were more than welcome. While I perfer the more atmospheric sounds of FC3BD, this still certainly is by no means bad stuff.

Conclusion: I am VERY glad I got this game on sale. If you catch it on sale and you're a fan of the retro aesthetic and synth music, or if you just want a narrative sequel to FC3BD, then I'd consider giving it a play. It was nice to be able to play the game just to see what it was like, but more than likely the only thing related to it that I will be revisiting is the soundtrack on YouTube.

name: Trials of the Blood Dragon
review:
Trials of the Blood Dragon is Ubisoft's attempt to tie more games into the Blood Dragon "series" that Far Cry 3's famed DLC tried to introduce. It's wacky, filled with nonsense and anime and explosions, and most interestingly does everything within the Trials engine - which really shows off just how much can be done with the creation tools. 

But, as a game, it's a bit boring. It has a lot of non-motorcycle segments and focuses a lot on the wacky story, and I both didn't feel compelled to go for higher medals, nor finish the game. It's unfortunately just not what I play Trials for. 

I respect it, but I don't recommend it.

name: Trials of the Blood Dragon
review:
Great aesthetics, wacky story, changes up the gameplay a lot (various vehicles, on foot, jetpack, with guns, highly varied environments, some with a twist) to keep things fresh. Great soundtrack.
For me the hardest and most annoying part are the controls on foot.
I played a lot of Trials HD on xbox360 back in the day, and must say this game is quite different, so don't expect a proper trials game. This feels more like a total conversion mod.
In a sale this game is a good deal for some hours of potentially quite challenging fun.
The story seems to progress no matter how shit you ride as long as you cross the finish line. To get the most of this game you should strive to get a good rating on the tracks you find enjoyable, so that means replaying them till you git gud

I knew there would be on-foot sections but after 4 missions in a row without using the bike once and fighting with the floaty unresponsive controls, the game becomes more of a slog through the rough sections.  The bike sections are great, as few and far between as they are, but the game just doesn't feel good otherwise.

4 TurboCranks out of 10

name: Trials of the Blood Dragon
review:
First of all, don't go into this expecting a pure Trials biking game since you only use the bike about a third of the time. The bike that you do get is pretty bottom tier as far as Trials bikes go, and the parts that don't involve the bike are a bit awkward and clumsy. However, the over the top nature of the game and the pure fun that can be had out of it makes up for most of these negatives since you can really see the experimentation in the track design and various abilities/modes of travel. No spoilers, but the story is kinda nuts, and it's a blast to see these wild tracks for the first time. 

I am recommending this for a casual playthrough, but not really for going for the A+ ranks on each track. This is because the goofy mechanics don't really lend themselves well for consistency and perfection, and you have to have some pretty solid runs to get the best rank. 

It's a lot of fun though if you put aside the fact that it's not really a traditional Trials game.

name: Trials of the Blood Dragon
review:
Blood Dragon infects another Ubisoft franchise.

Story: 

Rex Power Colt has settled and now has two kids Roxanne and Slayter. Just like their father 12 years before, they are tasked with a crazy task, take down the Empire of Vietnam and stop their forces and end Vietnam War 4. 

For fans of the original Blood Dragon, the story is about the same absolutely over the top and crazy experience, just a lot more toned down for a more younger audience, but the same type of humor is still here. I've seen people say that the new characters are a hit or miss, but I found them to be an enjoyable duo, although they do get on your nerves from time to time and I do think my man Rex is just the best. 

Graphics:

Visually Trials of the Blood Dragon oozes the same type of Neon barf that you expect when you hear the words Blood Dragon. Purple and Pink are the two primary colors and I have to say that I absolutely loved how vibrant all of the environments looked, my 80's aesthetic love has been pleased. 

A minor negative is that although there are vibrant colors, the lighting sometimes make certain environments look pretty rough, especially when darker lighting is introduced. It's sometimes even hard to see where you're supposed to go and it might end up getting you killed. 

Audio:

The developers knew that there was no point in not bringing back Power Glove and they came back with an absolute banger of a score. While the overall score isn't as memorable as the Far Cry Blood Dragon game, it's still filled with great and catchy synth tunes, that make the gameplay exhilarating. 

The voice acting is pretty decent overall, matches that saturday morning cartoon style very well and hearing some returning voice was a delight too. Sound effects for the bike and the explosions are also top tier and I wouldn't expect anything else from Trials. 

Gameplay:

Ever played a Trials game ? Well this is the same type of deal except now it's Blood Dragon themed. For those who are new, here's the rundown. Trials of the Blood Dragon is a physics based racing game where you're on a motocross bike and have to reach the end of the level as fast as possible without crashing. 

Trials of the Blood Dragon, when you play with the bike is an absolute blast and is just as good as when I originally used to play it when I was a kid. The controls are incredibly simple and the bike controls incredibly well, so when you're just blazing through each level, without crashing it's a blast.

However one of the newer additions of this particular game are the on stage segments and the different vehicle stages. Every once in a while you'll have to ditch the bike and do something with a different vehicle. While the actual on foot segments aren't particularly fun, they at least work and I had fun using the twitch shooter mechanics, however as soon as you get control of the jetpack the game becomes incredibly annoying. 

The controls of it are so finnicky and going fast isn't really an option because the controls are just so wonky. In one of the levels you also have a bomb attached to you and it's wobbling all over the place and I could never get the controls to work in my favor. It was just so annoying and all I would wish for is to get back on the bike. 

Verdict: 

Trials of the Blood Dragon is a bit wonky in areas, but when it's good it's great. I absolutely adore the style and I kind of wish Ubi gave out more weird spin offs and maybe that will happen one day, but as we stand today this is still a fun game and worth the time, just don't expect a Far Cry 3 Blood Dragon type experience.

Final Rating:
7.5/10

Pros:
+ Power Gloves score is amazing 
+ Great looking environments 
+ The bike levels are really fun 

Cons:
- Any levels that don't have the bike suck
- Lighting make certain areas look pretty bad 

If you liked this review please consider joining https://steamcommunity.com/groups/completingthebacklog and https://steamcommunity.com/groups/ImperialReviews

name: Trials of the Blood Dragon
review:
"Hey, did you watch all of the [i]Far Cry 3: Blood Dragon[/i] cutscenes like we told you to?"
"Yes sir, Ubisoft."
"Which one looked the most... interesting to you?"
"The sex scene. Why?"
"Funny you say that, because we want you to make an entire [i]Trials[/i] game based on that cutscene."
"...I'm not sure we follow."
"Continue the [i]Blood Dragon[/i] storyline where it left off after Rex defeats Sloan and the Omega Force. Rex gets married with Dr. Darling and they have two kids. One of them is named after a The Police song. The other just has a made-up name. Do what you will with the boy's name, but in the year 2019, there is a fourth Vietnam War, and they are sent to put an end to it. Just like [i]FC3:BD[/i], it should be as 80s and over-the-top as possible."
"I'm still not sure we follow."
"Just do it, Redlynx. We're sure that this game will be just as good as [i]FC3:BD[/i] and will totally not be forgettable at all."
"...yes sir, Ubisoft."

That's the conversation I imagine happened for the development of this game, and boy, was Ubisoft wrong.

As stated before, [i]Trials of the Blood Dragon[/i] continues from [i]Far Cry 3: Blood Dragon[/i]'s story, where Roxanne and Slayter, Rex "Power" Colt and Dr. Elizabeth Darling's children, are sent to shoot up some alleged communists and end Vietnam War 4. Yeah, two 12-year-olds going to war. Some of the plot points are ludicrous even for a sequel to a game based on well-known 80s films. [spoiler]Examples of such are that Dr. Darling is actually a Blood Dragon taking the form of a human, which makes Rox and Slay half-Blood Dragon. Dr. Darling disguises herself as General Ryback to help her kids kill the "communists," because while they could be communists, they're still just other humans standing in the way of the Dragornians, and the Killstar actually corrupts Rex so that it swings him to communism. That, I can understand though, because the Killstar in [i]FC3:BD[/i] used health as ammunition.[/spoiler] I wasn't much of a fan of the Saturday morning cartoon art style they were going for either. They completely butchered the Blood Dragons with it.

The gameplay is a mixed bag. While the APC and on foot sections weren't the worst things ever, the bike and minecart physics never felt all that great. Oftentimes I felt like I had the right alignment with the bike to meet the ground again, only to do an upside-down wheelie and kill myself. Minecarts share the same issue, only with added weight. The levels with the grappling hook bike and jetpack were absolutely miserable. The grappling hook is fun when it's first introduced, but in later levels, you really need to channel your inner Spider-Man to get past them, especially on Triangulation. As for the jetpack, Rox/Slay really wants to go full throttle with it even with the slightest tap of the W key, which makes it harder to turn and easier to undershoot and overshoot everything. In Extermination, where you not only have to deal with bad jetpack controls, you also have to transport a fragile, highly explosive bomb-probe to the mother bug, which does not help at all. What does help, though, is a forgiving amount of checkpoints across all levels. Even if you're not dead, you can still respawn at a checkpoint at any time if you know you're about to, and the respawn time is instant, so you don't have to wait to try again.

I also enjoyed the references to other franchises sprinkled throughout the game, even if some weren't from the 80s. There were allusions to [i]Hotline Miami[/i], [i]Halo[/i], [i]Star Wars[/i], [i]Super Mario Bros.[/i], and even [i]My Little Pony[/i]. Power Glove also made an amazing soundtrack for this game, but do you expect anything less from one of the greatest synthwave artists of all time (and where is "Clutch (Dragon Mix)" in the album?)?

Overall, [i]Trials of the Blood Dragon[/i] is a D game. Even if the plot is ridiculous for an 80s-based game, it's still charming, but gets overshadowed by crappy gameplay and physics. It's not a great sequel to [i]Far Cry 3: Blood Dragon[/i] and it's definitely not the [i]Hotline Miami 3[/i] we've been waiting for. Hopefully we can go back to the FPS genre for the next [i]Blood Dragon[/i] game. [spoiler]Maybe we won't even get another [i] Blood Dragon [/i] game at all since Rex, Dr. Darling, Rox, and Slay all killed each other in the true ending.[/spoiler]

name: Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic
review:
I do recommend this game but with a MAJOR Caveat. It Can be a pain in the butt to implement.

I run windows 8.1 on a pretty powerful gaming PC 

Bought this game as part of a package deal with SH3 & SH4  Both of those work great right after downloading.  SH5 can be a dud. It REQUIRES an internet update program be running when you launch the game (at least once) That program has caused problems for countless people. (Really, I didn't count them!) 

I've watched the game in you-tube let's play videos and would really love to try it but so far all attempts at remediating the problems (even following long checklists on subsim.com) have given me no joy. 


I did FINALLY get the game to play. All I had to do was...

Report problem to Ubisoft and plead for help there and in the subsim.com boards, then...

1) Uninstall the Ubisoft Game Launcher from the Control Panel
2) Go to Program (x86) folder and deleted the Ubisoft Folder containing the Game Launcher Folder
3) Run Registry Cleaner & Reboot the system
4) Down-load a new Game Launcher from here:
http://forums.ubi.com/showthread.php/675733-Ubisoft-Game-Launcher-Download-Forums
5) Reinstall the Game launcher
6) Go to the Game Launcher folder & Open the folder and go to the 1st Game Launcher .exe application icon and run as administrator (disabling the proxy option)
7) Enter my validation code and got an ubisoft screen with Play Silent Hunter 5 prominently featured in the center .

8) Go to the Steam/SteamApps/Common/SilentHunter5 folder and 
Rightclick on SH5.exe then choose Compatibilty Tab then set the X on "Disable scaling on High DPI) worked like a dream. (just for good measure I also ticked the box about "run as admin")

Easy Peasy! Now why didn't I do that right the first time?

As to the game-play itself. Life aboard a submarine was tough. This game simulates that. Many may find gameplay to be repetetive at points and at other otherpoints very uneventful. If you're like me you may get just as much enjoyment out of watching others play (hopefully with the boring bits cut out) as from playing it yourself.

name: Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic
review:
I didn't have a problem running the game, not sure why other people are having issues. The only mention of uPlay is when it synchronizes saves online.

As for the game itself:

For people looking for some casual submarine fun I do NOT recommend this game, cold waters is better for that kind of experience. SH5 has issues that require some extra effort to work around, it's by no means a plug and play game.

For people looking for a hardcore u-boat simulation experience this game is a must have, but not in its vanilla state. The vanilla experience is watered down in comparison to SH4, but there are a ton of mods available through subsim.com which make it a great successor to SH4, especially if you're sick of / not particularly enthralled by the restrictive interactions in SH4. You'll find yourself wandering throughout the entire length of your ship throughout each of your patrols and it really plays into a sense of immersion that no other game offers. If you don't want to worry about compiling a list of mods, installing them correctly etc I would recommend the Wolves of Steel modpack which introduces a ton of high quality mods which greatly enhance the immersion and overall experience, but at the cost of a somewhat steep learning curve. I've spent dozens of hours trying to master my use of the various instruments and measurements to compile a correct torpedo solution to the point where i can usually hit about half of all the torpedoes I fire, but I'm still constantly improving. Steam says i've spent 20 hours playing but I had to take the game offline for a couple mods which require some more extensive tweaking, I'd estimate my play time to be over 100 hours as of the time of this review and it inspired me to spend another 50 hours studying WWII submarine warfare and torpedo solutions to improve my tonnage each patrol. If you're a hardcore warfare sim fan who fancies the idea of diving to excessive depths to avoid depth charges, silently sneaking in among the ranks of a massive escorted convoy in the dead of night, or straining every last watt out of your batteries to move into a mined harbor unseen to take out Royal Navy battleships, then this is the game for you, and for $10 it's hard to go wrong.

Tip: in the campaign selection screen type "silentotto" to unlock all the missions. I haven't the patience to complete an entire mission just yet as each spans the course of a year or more in game time, and from what I've heard there's some issues with the game counting your progress in a particular mission to complete it so if you're looking for some variety after a few long patrols you can explore some of the other campaign missions, each having some unique challenges of their own.

name: Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic
review:
I know, I know ...

The game is busted.  It doesn't work.  Bugs all over the place.  Don't buy it ...

... and so on, and so forth.

However, in years past, I've played this game for hours on end, without any bugs, no CTDs, nothing.  So, how did I do it?

Mods.  Mods are the key to just about every game from Ubisoft (which is not one of my favorite game publishers, but they do have some great ideas).  Specifically, the Wolves of Steel (TWoS), which can be found at SubSim.com.  How good is it?  At times, you would swear that you are the Kapitanleutnant in the movie "Das Boot".  I've devoured that movie (including the 5-hour version of it), and I love TWoS when connected with Silent Hunter 5.

You start the campaign game in the Eastern Baltic, at a port which was Hitler's last bloodless conquest.  War has just broken out against Poland.  At least, it is just about to break out:  the date is August 31, 1939, and there is the Polish Navy to deal with.

After you clear the port, you go on an inspection tour of your "Boot" - short for "Unterseeboot".  You climb down the ladder into the conning tower, and then into the control (while your crew stands watch on the bridge).  You speak to the navigator, the Chief, and go forward through the hatch and speak to your radio operator and sound man.  If you turn around, you'll see your own cabin (a tiny little place with a curtain, your "desk", and your bunk).  Just forward of that location in the ward room, some crew quarters, and the torpedo room.  Going aft, you find some more crew quarters, the tiny galley (which was supposed to prepare food for 53 crewmen), the engine room, and the electric motor room.

If you've ever watched "Das Boot", all of these locations look eerily familiar (and by the way, I've toured the U-555 which is currently on display at the Museum of Science and Industry, in Chicago.

As you continue your operations against the Polish, the British, and all of Germany's enemies (later to include the United States), and you are pursued by aircraft and destroyers, you may feel a surge of adrenaline, increased heart rate ... you feel like you are there.  The game gets harder, as the years pass, and if you survive.

The graphics are beautiful, the gameplay is seamless, and it functions like Ubisoft SHOULD HAVE made it function.

To make it work right, you simply have to get the Wolves of Steel mod.  It's free.  And the basic game costs only $10, so what have you got to lose?

If you have any interest at all, in WW2 naval warfare, and specifically in the U-boat campaign in the Atlantic, you simply have to buy this game.

Strongly recommended ....

After-Action report:
Just two nights ago, I guided my Type VIIb boat into the Gulf of Danzig (using The Wolves of Steel megamod), and found a Polish taskforce consisting of two destroyers.  Although I approached them with decks awash (to lower my profile on the horizon) a lookout must have seen us.  The bottom was 67 meters, so I went to 50 meters depth and went silent.

Depth charges were all over the place, and we got shook up pretty badly, but took no serious damage.  Suddenly, the depth charge attacks ended.  I judged that the destroyers had run out them, and took my boat up to periscope depth.  Carefully edging the attack scope up, till it just broke the surface.  I saw one of the destroyers about 500 meters off.  I made a quick resolution of the attack and sent two torpedoes its way, aiming just below the bridge.  Both hit, and the destroyer went down quickly.

Meanwhile, the other destroyer started to run away, but he was very close, also.  I made a snapshot with tube 3 & 4, and one of them hit.  When tube one was loaded, I hit him, again.  He went down very fast. 

I'm now on my way to my new base, at Kiel.  In the interim, I sank two Polish merchant vessels, one with a torpedo, and one with gunfire.

name: Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic
review:
Wow I'm going to have to fight hard here but I have a sincere review that reveals that actual enormous VALUE this game adds to the series (if it was introduceable enough) I will.

I will say one thing about this game. Fighting and traveling are PERFECT IN THIS GAME. They are beyond, without a doubt, the most perfect controls for not only point and click traveling with time dilation adventure, but also precision like freaking Nazi astronauts. Fighting is actually BETTER than Silent Hunter Four, and I know I'll get flak for that, but for how in depth and satisfying with how the controls are in this game, without a doubt these are the most intuitive feeling controls with a torpedo submarine is "shoot it straight and then a little right and left" is such a perfectly simple control movement with this game. They must have poured 99% of their time into that. Even if it's not perfect pistons and sheers, it still works and requires judgement and skill.

What is WEIRD is the walking, and let me go beyond that, who the fuck is this german guy waking you up in the very beginning? This guy sees your eyes are open and just keeps himself in your face for too long asking if you're awake, like the fuck man? Then he skips down the corridor and uncomfortably massages the backs of people he sees, and I'm going to be honest it was fine, but the lack of body animations the rest of the crew had, it was a pretty bad scene. Going beyond that, the interior play of the game is a cool attempt and does something new but does also fail to really add any play value and actually breaks the game's flow up a little. 

I will say now, if I could just 'edit' the flow of the game from start to finish it's actually a 9.5 huge contribution to its genre /10. It performs leaps and bounds which this game has really done for itself. If one read a novel like Operation Drumbeat: U-Boat 123, this is the perfect game to have modding capabilities, or really just altered start dates to feel the soldiery of a sailor in African Germany, and that's where I'd spend my effort if the company actually hired me. Again, great game, too old to fix (maybe), good potential on many things except scary weird start of the game.

name: Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic
review:
TL:DR from a 15 year Silent Hunter 3 veteran: 
-Skipped vanilla and went straight for The Wolves Of Steel mod.
Game might be up your alley if you like to fiddle around and get things exactly how you want them the hard way but it's a fight and like me, you might not have the patience to spend time fixing Ubisofts mistakes.
I'm going back to Silent Hunter 3.

-Game has improvements over SH3 like graphics and crew interactions but many things have been made worse like the UI and all the keyboard shortcuts however to set controls to SH3 ones you need a mod which if you like WASD movement still isn't good enough since it uses the arrow keys.
-Most issues can be fixed with mods but many mods are very complex like NewUIs with TDC for SH5 by TheDarkWraith version 7.4.2 , I'm well able to fiddle around with this stuff and get it to a point where I'd be happy but I don't have the patience to mess around with that when I just want an idiot proof slap it together and boom it works solution.
-Crew animations are somewhat janky like for example, always climbing ladders at a 90 degree angle from what looks normal.(BIG sin considering Ubisoft had amazing animations in 2 Assassins Creed games prior to SH5's release.)

Suffice it to say there's enough issues that I'm going back to the tried and true Silent Hunter 3.  I've never played Silent Hunter 4 but Americans measuring depth in feet instead of proper meters like everyone else is a no from me here in Ireland.

name: Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic
review:
Used to be acceptable (once modded) to play. Now it's been removed from Ubisoft's supported games and so can't be launched directly from Steam as it never connects (there's nothing to connect to). 

It is possible to download Ubisoft Connect from the Ubisoft site directly which technically allows you to bypass this, however it demands the CD Key (options for game on Steam has this), however, when you enter it, it says it's already registered with another account and you still can't play it. 

Absolutely disgusting that customers who've paid money for a product can no longer use it, and even worse that Steam are STILL selling the 'game'.

Ahoy, submarine enthusiasts! Silent Hunter 5®: Battle of the Atlantic is your ticket to commanding a German U-boat in the most thrilling underwater warfare experience ever crafted. In summary, Silent Hunter 5®: Battle of the Atlantic is a must-play for fans of submarine simulations and naval history alike. It’s a grand voyage into the silent depths of the ocean where your strategic prowess and command skills will be put to the ultimate test. Dive in and take command! 🌊⚓🎮

name: Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic
review:
Cannot reccomend this game why?

This awesome game is tied to Ubisoft uplay/ubisoft connect. To get the game to run you will need to 

1. download Ubisoft connect 

2. uninstall uplay 

3. Sign into or make a Ubisoft account

4. Take your steam game key and redeem it on Ubisoft launcher 

5. You can now play this game on steam

Seems easy enough right? Well Ubisoft is tanking faster right now than a merchant ship hit with a spread of 4 torpedos. Some think they will either go bankrupt or be bought out by another company in the coming years some say as early as 2025 but it’s just speculation.

 If that happens for however long you will presumably not be able to play this game due to it needing to connect to Ubisoft servers to launch the game. 

You can kind of see this Happening with other Ubisoft titles from around this time with similar DRM measures. Like Assasins creed 2 where you cannot play paid dlc anymore due to servers for that being shut down around 2022. Or games like The crew no longer being playable at all. Or farcry 3 and assassins creed 3 where dlc cant be played anymore

If the servers go down you simply you won’t be able to play your game anymore  

(This is more or less a buyer beware your game could potentially become unplayable in the near future. Or atleast unplayable for an unknown period of time if Ubisoft goes bankrupt/closes down/sells its company off)
